questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of daily productive cough with copious foul-smelling sputum. She has been hospitalized twice for pneumonia in the past year. She has a history of cystic fibrosis diagnosed in childhood. High-resolution CT of the chest shows dilated bronchi with thickened walls where the bronchial diameter exceeds the diameter of the adjacent pulmonary artery. Which of the following best describes this CT finding?

explanation.summary: The signet ring sign on CT describes a dilated bronchus (the ring) adjacent to its companion pulmonary artery (the stone), with the bronchial diameter exceeding the arterial diameter. This is a hallmark radiologic finding of bronchiectasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In bronchiectasis, chronic inflammation destroys the bronchial wall cartilage, leading to irreversible dilation. On cross-section CT, the dilated bronchus paired with its smaller companion artery creates the classic signet ring sign, which is highly suggestive of bronchiectasis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Crazy-paving pattern describes thickened interlobular septa superimposed on ground-glass opacities and is associated with pulmonary alveolar proteinosis or other diffuse lung diseases, not bronchiectasis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ground-glass opacities represent a hazy increase in lung density that does not obscure the underlying bronchovascular structures. They are seen in many conditions such as interstitial lung disease and infection but do not describe the bronchial dilation seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Honeycombing refers to clustered cystic airspaces with thick walls in a subpleural distribution, indicating end-stage fibrotic lung disease such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tree-in-bud pattern describes centrilobular branching nodular opacities resembling a budding tree, typically seen in infectious bronchiolitis or endobronchial spread of tuberculosis.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with a history of recurrent sinusitis and chronic productive cough since childhood is evaluated for infertility. Physical examination reveals dextrocardia. A chest CT scan shows bilateral lower lobe bronchiectasis. Nasal mucosal biopsy reveals immotile cilia with absent dynein arms on electron microscopy. Which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Kartagener syndrome is a subtype of primary ciliary dyskinesia characterized by the triad of situs inversus (including dextrocardia), bronchiectasis, and chronic sinusitis. It is caused by mutations affecting dynein, rendering cilia immotile.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of dextrocardia (situs inversus), bronchiectasis, chronic sinusitis, infertility, and absent dynein arms on electron microscopy is pathognomonic for Kartagener syndrome. Dynein is essential for ciliary motility; its absence impairs mucociliary clearance and also disrupts normal organ laterality during embryonic development.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis causes bronchiectasis with elevated IgE and eosinophilia in patients with asthma or CF. It does not cause situs inversus or ciliary dynein defects.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Common variable immunodeficiency can cause bronchiectasis from recurrent infections due to hypogammaglobulinemia, but it does not cause situs inversus or ciliary ultrastructural defects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cystic fibrosis causes bronchiectasis through defective chloride transport and thickened mucus. It does not cause situs inversus or abnormal dynein arms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Young syndrome features bronchiectasis, sinusitis, and azoospermia but does not involve situs inversus or structural ciliary defects on electron microscopy.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a long history of bronchiectasis develops worsening dyspnea on exertion, lower-extremity edema, and hepatomegaly. Echocardiography reveals right ventricular dilation with an estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 55 mmHg. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of her cardiac findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aortic valve stenosis causing left ventricular pressure overload

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left atrial myxoma causing mitral valve obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulmonary capillary compression increasing right ventricular afterload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tricuspid valve endocarditis causing valvular regurgitation

explanation.summary: Chronic bronchiectasis can lead to pulmonary hypertension through pulmonary capillary compression and hypoxic vasoconstriction, increasing right ventricular afterload and eventually causing cor pulmonale.
explanation.whyCorrect: In bronchiectasis, chronic inflammation and destruction of the lung parenchyma compress pulmonary capillaries and cause hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction. This raises pulmonary artery pressures, increasing right ventricular afterload and leading to right ventricular dilation and failure (cor pulmonale), which explains her edema and hepatomegaly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic valve stenosis causes left ventricular pressure overload and would present with left-sided heart failure symptoms such as pulmonary edema, not isolated right-sided findings.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Left atrial myxoma would cause symptoms mimicking mitral stenosis with pulmonary congestion and would be visible as an intracardiac mass on echocardiography.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Systemic hypertension affects the left ventricle. This patient's findings are isolated to the right heart with elevated pulmonary artery pressures, indicating a pulmonary rather than systemic cause.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tricuspid valve endocarditis would show valvular vegetations on echocardiography and typically presents with septic pulmonary emboli, not with pulmonary hypertension from chronic lung disease.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with bronchiectasis presents with increased sputum production, fever, and worsening dyspnea for 3 days. His most recent sputum culture from 2 months ago grew Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Which of the following is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic therapy?

explanation.summary: In bronchiectasis patients with a history of Pseudomonas on prior sputum culture, empiric therapy for acute exacerbations should cover Pseudomonas. Piperacillin-tazobactam is an appropriate antipseudomonal agent.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a patient with bronchiectasis has a prior sputum culture growing Pseudomonas, the exacerbation treatment should be tailored to cover this organism. Piperacillin-tazobactam has excellent antipseudomonal activity and is an appropriate choice, along with other options such as carbapenems or aminoglycosides.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amoxicillin is appropriate for bronchiectasis exacerbations caused by sensitive organisms such as non-beta-lactamase-producing H. influenzae but has no clinically meaningful activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Azithromycin is used as prophylactic therapy to reduce exacerbation frequency in bronchiectasis but has limited activity against Pseudomonas and is not appropriate as primary treatment for an acute Pseudomonas exacerbation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Doxycycline covers many respiratory pathogens but does not have reliable antipseudomonal activity and would be inadequate given the prior Pseudomonas culture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Metronidazole covers anaerobic organisms and is used for intra-abdominal and certain CNS infections. It has no activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a 10-year history of bronchiectasis undergoes pulmonary function testing. Which of the following spirometry findings is most consistent with her diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased FEV1 with decreased FEV1/FVC ratio

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased FEV1 with increased FEV1/FVC ratio

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased TLC with normal FEV1/FVC ratio

explanation.summary: Bronchiectasis causes obstructive lung disease. Spirometry characteristically shows a decreased FEV1 and a reduced FEV1/FVC ratio, with a normal or increased TLC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bronchiectasis is classified as an obstructive lung disease because the dilated, mucus-filled airways impair expiratory airflow. Spirometry demonstrates a reduced FEV1 and a decreased FEV1/FVC ratio, which is the hallmark pattern of obstructive pulmonary disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A decreased FEV1 with an increased FEV1/FVC ratio would suggest a restrictive pattern where FVC is disproportionately reduced. Bronchiectasis is obstructive, not restrictive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A decreased TLC with a normal FEV1/FVC ratio describes a restrictive lung disease pattern. In bronchiectasis, TLC is typically normal or increased due to air trapping.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An increased FEV1 is not consistent with any form of obstructive or restrictive lung disease. Bronchiectasis causes airflow limitation with decreased FEV1.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A normal FEV1 with decreased FVC could suggest restriction. In bronchiectasis, the FEV1 is decreased and the FEV1/FVC ratio is reduced.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis and bronchiectasis has been prescribed a nebulized medication that cleaves extracellular DNA in airway secretions to reduce mucus viscosity. Which of the following medications was most likely prescribed?

explanation.summary: Dornase alfa is a recombinant human DNase that cleaves extracellular DNA released from degenerating neutrophils in the airways, thereby reducing mucus viscosity and improving airflow in patients with cystic fibrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In cystic fibrosis, thick airway mucus contains large amounts of DNA from degenerating neutrophils. Dornase alfa is a recombinant DNase enzyme that cleaves this extracellular DNA, reducing mucus viscosity and improving mucociliary clearance and pulmonary function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acetylcysteine is also a mucolytic agent, but it works by breaking disulfide bonds in mucoproteins through its free sulfhydryl group, not by cleaving DNA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Albuterol is a short-acting beta-2 agonist bronchodilator that relaxes airway smooth muscle. It does not have mucolytic properties.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ipratropium is an anticholinergic bronchodilator that reduces bronchospasm by blocking muscarinic receptors. It does not cleave DNA or reduce mucus viscosity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tobramycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic used in inhaled form for chronic Pseudomonas suppression in CF patients. It has antibacterial, not mucolytic, properties.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with non-cystic-fibrosis bronchiectasis presents with an acute exacerbation characterized by increased purulent sputum and low-grade fever. He has not had a sputum culture performed in the past year. Which of the following is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic?

explanation.summary: When a patient with bronchiectasis has no recent sputum culture data to guide therapy, a fluoroquinolone such as levofloxacin is recommended as empiric treatment for acute exacerbations due to its broad-spectrum coverage including common respiratory pathogens.
explanation.whyCorrect: In bronchiectasis patients without recent sputum culture data, guidelines recommend a respiratory fluoroquinolone such as levofloxacin as empiric therapy. It provides broad coverage against H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, and Pseudomonas, covering the most common organisms encountered in bronchiectasis exacerbations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is appropriate when sputum cultures show non-pseudomonal beta-lactamase-positive organisms such as M. catarrhalis, but it is not the first-line choice when no culture data is available.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Clindamycin primarily covers gram-positive organisms and anaerobes and has limited activity against the gram-negative organisms that commonly cause bronchiectasis exacerbations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Meropenem is a carbapenem reserved for patients with known Pseudomonas or multi-drug resistant infections. It is overly broad for empiric use without culture data suggesting resistant organisms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vancomycin covers gram-positive organisms including MRSA but has no activity against gram-negative pathogens like H. influenzae and Pseudomonas, which are the predominant organisms in bronchiectasis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a history of recurrent pneumonias presents with chronic productive cough producing large volumes of purulent sputum daily. Chest X-ray reveals parallel linear opacities in the lower lobes consistent with thickened bronchial walls. Which of the following terms best describes this radiographic finding?

explanation.summary: Tram-track lines on chest X-ray represent dilated, thickened parallel bronchial walls seen in longitudinal section and are a classic radiographic finding of bronchiectasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tram-tracking (tram-track lines) refers to the appearance of dilated bronchi with thickened walls seen in longitudinal section on chest X-ray. The parallel linear opacities resemble railroad tracks and are characteristic of bronchiectasis. When air-filled, the space between the lines appears translucent; when mucus-filled, it appears opaque.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Air bronchograms are air-filled bronchi visible within opacified (consolidated) lung parenchyma, typically seen in pneumonia or atelectasis, not in bronchiectasis specifically.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Kerley B lines are thin horizontal lines at the lung periphery caused by thickened interlobular septa, seen in conditions such as pulmonary edema and lymphangitic carcinomatosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Plate-like atelectasis appears as linear densities usually at the lung bases due to subsegmental collapse, not the parallel linear opacities of thickened bronchial walls.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A reticulonodular pattern consists of overlapping reticular and nodular opacities and is associated with interstitial lung disease, not the dilated airways of bronchiectasis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with bronchiectasis secondary to childhood pertussis infection presents with sudden-onset massive hemoptysis. Bronchoscopy fails to control the bleeding. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lung resection of the affected segment

explanation.summary: Massive hemoptysis refractory to bronchoscopic intervention in bronchiectasis may require surgical lung resection to control life-threatening bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Massive hemoptysis is a serious complication of bronchiectasis resulting from erosion of bronchial arteries. When bronchoscopic interventions (such as laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy) fail, surgical resection of the affected lung segment is indicated to control the hemorrhage and prevent death.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chest physiotherapy helps with chronic mucus clearance but is contraindicated during active massive hemoptysis because percussion could worsen the bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Systemic corticosteroids treat inflammatory conditions but do not address the vascular source of massive hemoptysis and would not control acute life-threatening bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nebulized hypertonic saline is used to improve mucus clearance in chronic management. It has no role in the acute management of massive hemoptysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prophylactic azithromycin is used for long-term suppression of recurrent exacerbations but does not address acute hemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis treated with methotrexate presents with chronic cough productive of thick sputum and recurrent lower respiratory tract infections over the past 2 years. A high-resolution CT of the chest shows bronchial dilation in the lower lobes bilaterally. Which of the following mechanisms best explains the development of bronchiectasis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspiration of gastric contents from GERD

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immotile cilia from dynein arm mutation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased mucus viscosity from DNA accumulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Systemic inflammatory disease with immune suppression

explanation.summary: Rheumatoid arthritis is a systemic inflammatory disorder that can cause bronchiectasis both through the inflammatory disease process itself and through the immunosuppressive effects of its treatment, which increase susceptibility to recurrent pulmonary infections.
explanation.whyCorrect: Systemic inflammatory diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis are recognized causes of bronchiectasis. The autoimmune inflammation directly damages airways, and immunosuppressive therapies like methotrexate further increase vulnerability to recurrent respiratory infections, which perpetuate the cycle of airway damage and dilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While aspiration from GERD can cause airway inflammation and bronchiectasis, there is no indication of GERD symptoms in this patient. Her rheumatoid arthritis and immunosuppression are the most likely contributors.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Defective chloride channel transport is the mechanism of cystic fibrosis, which presents in childhood and is a genetic disorder, not an acquired inflammatory condition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immotile cilia from dynein arm mutations cause primary ciliary dyskinesia and Kartagener syndrome, presenting with situs inversus and symptoms from birth, not in a 52-year-old with RA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DNA accumulation in mucus is primarily a feature of cystic fibrosis, where neutrophil-derived DNA thickens airway secretions. This mechanism is not the primary driver of bronchiectasis in inflammatory arthritis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with asthma presents with worsening cough, brown mucus plugs, and low-grade fever. Laboratory studies show a serum IgE level of 2500 IU/mL (normal <100) and peripheral eosinophilia. Chest CT reveals central bronchiectasis. Skin prick testing is positive for Aspergillus fumigatus. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) is a hypersensitivity response to Aspergillus fumigatus that occurs in patients with asthma or CF. It is characterized by markedly elevated IgE, eosinophilia, central bronchiectasis, and mucus plugging.
explanation.whyCorrect: ABPA is diagnosed by the combination of asthma, markedly elevated serum IgE (>1000 IU/mL), peripheral eosinophilia, positive Aspergillus skin testing, and central bronchiectasis on CT. The hyperimmune response activates Th2 cells, recruits eosinophils, and causes intense airway inflammation leading to mucus plug formation and bronchiectasis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An aspergilloma is a fungus ball that colonizes a pre-existing lung cavity, typically in patients with prior TB or sarcoidosis. It does not cause elevated IgE, eosinophilia, or central bronchiectasis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Churg-Strauss syndrome (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) causes asthma and eosinophilia but is a systemic vasculitis with extrapulmonary manifestations including neuropathy and skin lesions, not central bronchiectasis with Aspergillus sensitization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis occurs in severely immunocompromised patients and presents with tissue invasion, necrosis, and nodular infiltrates, not central bronchiectasis with eosinophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Loeffler syndrome is a transient pulmonary eosinophilia typically caused by parasitic infections, presenting with migratory pulmonary infiltrates, not central bronchiectasis with Aspergillus sensitization.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 15-year history of bronchiectasis requires chronic suppressive antibiotic therapy due to frequent Pseudomonas aeruginosa exacerbations. Which of the following prophylactic regimens is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: For patients with bronchiectasis who have frequent Pseudomonas exacerbations, prophylactic inhaled tobramycin is commonly used for chronic suppressive therapy, often alternating with oral macrolides such as azithromycin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inhaled tobramycin provides local antipseudomonal activity directly to the airways while minimizing systemic side effects. It is a well-established option for chronic suppressive therapy in bronchiectasis patients with recurrent Pseudomonas infections, reducing bacterial burden and exacerbation frequency.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Oral amoxicillin has no clinically meaningful activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa and would be ineffective for suppression of Pseudomonas-driven exacerbations.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oral cephalexin is a first-generation cephalosporin that covers gram-positive cocci and some community gram-negatives but has no antipseudomonal activity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oral metronidazole covers anaerobic bacteria and certain parasites but has no activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole has variable activity against some gram-negative organisms but is not a reliable antipseudomonal agent and is not used for Pseudomonas prophylaxis.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman is diagnosed with bronchiectasis after years of chronic productive cough. Her physician explains that the disease involves a self-perpetuating cycle. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic mechanism that maintains this cycle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Airway dilation improves mucus clearance, reducing infection risk

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bronchial cartilage hypertrophy increases airway wall rigidity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Impaired mucus clearance promotes recurrent infection and further airway damage

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased surfactant production leads to alveolar instability

choices.label: E
choices.text: Progressive fibrosis of the alveolar septa restricts chest wall expansion

explanation.summary: Bronchiectasis is maintained by a vicious cycle: chronic inflammation and infection destroy bronchial wall cartilage, causing permanent dilation that impairs mucus clearance, which in turn promotes further infection and inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pathophysiology of bronchiectasis involves a self-perpetuating cycle. Infection or inflammation damages the bronchial walls, causing irreversible dilation. The dilated, damaged airways cannot effectively clear mucus, creating a favorable environment for bacterial colonization and recurrent infections, which cause further structural damage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Airway dilation in bronchiectasis paradoxically worsens mucus clearance rather than improving it, because the structural damage to the bronchial walls impairs the normal mucociliary clearance mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bronchiectasis involves destruction, not hypertrophy, of bronchial cartilage. The weakened cartilage leads to airway wall collapse and dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surfactant is produced by type II pneumocytes in the alveoli and maintains alveolar stability. Bronchiectasis affects the bronchi, not the alveoli or surfactant production.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fibrosis of alveolar septa describes restrictive interstitial lung disease. Bronchiectasis is an obstructive disease affecting the airways, not the alveolar septa.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with chronic bronchiectasis is admitted for an acute exacerbation. Sputum culture from 6 weeks ago grew Haemophilus influenzae that was sensitive to amoxicillin. Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic for this exacerbation?

explanation.summary: When a recent sputum culture identifies a sensitive organism such as non-beta-lactamase-producing H. influenzae, targeted therapy with amoxicillin is appropriate for the acute exacerbation of bronchiectasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Guidelines recommend tailoring antibiotic choice to recent sputum culture results. Since the recent culture grew H. influenzae sensitive to amoxicillin, this narrow-spectrum agent is the most appropriate choice, providing effective coverage while minimizing unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aztreonam is a monobactam used for Pseudomonas and other gram-negative infections. It is overly broad for a sensitive H. influenzae infection and is reserved for patients with prior Pseudomonas cultures.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Levofloxacin is recommended when no recent sputum culture data is available. With known sensitivity to amoxicillin, a narrower-spectrum agent is preferred to reduce resistance development.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Meropenem is a broad-spectrum carbapenem reserved for multi-drug resistant organisms. Using it for amoxicillin-sensitive H. influenzae promotes unnecessary resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Piperacillin-tazobactam is an antipseudomonal agent reserved for patients with prior Pseudomonas cultures. It is unnecessarily broad for a sensitive H. influenzae infection.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman with primary ciliary dyskinesia has chronic productive cough and recurrent sinusitis. She is interested in non-pharmacologic strategies to improve her respiratory symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Strict bed rest to reduce sputum production

explanation.summary: Chest physiotherapy using mechanical devices is a cornerstone of nonpharmacologic management for bronchiectasis, helping to mobilize and clear thickened airway secretions that patients cannot adequately clear due to impaired mucociliary function.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chest physiotherapy, including postural drainage, percussion, and use of oscillatory positive expiratory pressure devices, is a key nonpharmacologic intervention in bronchiectasis. These techniques help loosen and expel thick mucus, reducing the risk of infections and improving respiratory function in patients with impaired clearance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Physical activity is encouraged in bronchiectasis patients as exercise helps mobilize secretions and improves overall cardiopulmonary fitness. Avoidance of activity would be detrimental.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhaled corticosteroids are pharmacologic, not nonpharmacologic, therapy. They may be used in some bronchiectasis patients with concurrent asthma but are not the primary management strategy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Continuous oxygen therapy is not indicated unless there is documented resting hypoxemia. It is a pharmacologic intervention and not a routine recommendation for all bronchiectasis patients.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Strict bed rest would worsen secretion retention and deconditioning. Mobilization and airway clearance techniques are recommended to improve mucus clearance.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with bronchiectasis develops partial collapse of the left lower lobe. He has worsening dyspnea and decreased breath sounds over the left base. Chest X-ray confirms left lower lobe opacification with volume loss. Which of the following complications of bronchiectasis best explains this finding?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cor pulmonale from right heart failure

explanation.summary: Atelectasis (partial or complete lung collapse) is a recognized complication of bronchiectasis caused by thick mucus plugs obstructing the airway and preventing ventilation of the distal lung parenchyma.
explanation.whyCorrect: In bronchiectasis, excessive thick mucus can form plugs that completely obstruct a bronchus. The air distal to the obstruction is absorbed, causing collapse (atelectasis) of the affected lobe or segment. The chest X-ray shows opacification with volume loss, which is characteristic of obstructive atelectasis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cor pulmonale from right heart failure presents with peripheral edema, hepatomegaly, and elevated jugular venous pressure, not focal lobar collapse with volume loss.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pleural effusion would appear as fluid layering in the costophrenic angle with a meniscus sign, not as lobar opacification with volume loss.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pneumothorax would show hyperlucency and absent lung markings, the opposite of the opacification with volume loss described here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary embolism typically presents with acute dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and hypoxemia but does not cause lobar opacification with volume loss on chest X-ray.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man who immigrated from Southeast Asia 20 years ago presents with a 2-year history of chronic cough with blood-streaked sputum. He recalls being treated for a pulmonary infection in his youth. Chest CT shows upper lobe bronchiectasis with cavitary lesions and calcified hilar lymph nodes. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of his bronchiectasis?

explanation.summary: The combination of upper lobe bronchiectasis, cavitary lesions, and calcified hilar lymph nodes in a patient from a TB-endemic region with a history of a prior pulmonary infection strongly suggests post-tuberculous bronchiectasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tuberculosis is a major infectious cause of bronchiectasis worldwide. Severe TB infection causes tissue destruction, cavitation, and scarring predominantly in the upper lobes. This ongoing structural damage impairs mucociliary clearance and leads to bronchiectasis. Calcified hilar lymph nodes indicate prior granulomatous infection consistent with TB.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency causes panacinar emphysema predominantly in the lower lobes, not upper lobe bronchiectasis with cavitary lesions and calcified lymph nodes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspiration pneumonia typically affects the dependent portions of the lungs (posterior segments of upper lobes, superior segments of lower lobes in recumbent patients) and does not cause calcified hilar lymph nodes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Foreign body aspiration causes localized bronchiectasis distal to the obstruction but would not produce bilateral upper lobe disease with calcified hilar lymph nodes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sjögren syndrome can cause bronchiectasis through increased mucus viscosity and immune-mediated airway damage, but it does not cause cavitary lesions or calcified hilar lymph nodes.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with bronchiectasis has been using nebulized hypertonic saline as part of her chronic management. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which hypertonic saline improves mucus clearance?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Creates an osmotic gradient drawing water into the airway

choices.label: D
choices.text: Directly kills bacteria in the airway lumen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stimulates surfactant secretion by type II pneumocytes

explanation.summary: Nebulized hypertonic saline creates an osmotic gradient that draws water from the airway epithelial cells into the airway lumen, diluting and thinning the mucus to facilitate its clearance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypertonic saline increases the osmolality of the airway surface liquid relative to the epithelial cell interior. This osmotic gradient causes water to flow into the airway lumen, hydrating the mucus and making it less viscous and easier to expectorate, thereby improving mucociliary clearance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Breaking disulfide bonds in mucoproteins is the mechanism of action of acetylcysteine, which exerts its mucolytic effect through its free sulfhydryl group, not through osmotic effects.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cleaving extracellular DNA is the mechanism of dornase alfa (recombinant DNase). Hypertonic saline does not have enzymatic activity against DNA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While high concentrations of salt can be bacteriostatic, the primary therapeutic mechanism of nebulized hypertonic saline in bronchiectasis is airway hydration and mucus thinning, not direct antibacterial action.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Surfactant is produced by type II alveolar cells and reduces alveolar surface tension. Hypertonic saline acts on the airway mucus layer, not on surfactant production.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman with a history of Sjögren syndrome presents with recurrent lower respiratory tract infections and daily productive cough for the past 3 years. CT of the chest confirms bronchiectasis. Which of the following best explains how Sjögren syndrome predisposes to bronchiectasis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune destruction of alveolar type I cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defective neutrophil chemotaxis to the airways

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased mucus viscosity from impaired glandular secretion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced alpha-1 antitrypsin leading to protease-antiprotease imbalance

explanation.summary: Sjögren syndrome is an autoimmune disorder that targets exocrine glands, causing decreased and thickened secretions. In the airways, this increases mucus viscosity, impairing mucociliary clearance and predisposing to recurrent infections and bronchiectasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sjögren syndrome involves autoimmune destruction and dysfunction of exocrine glands, including the submucosal glands of the bronchi. This results in decreased fluid secretion and increased mucus viscosity in the airways, impairing normal mucociliary clearance and creating conditions favorable for recurrent infections that lead to bronchiectasis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sjögren syndrome targets exocrine glands, not alveolar type I cells. Alveolar cell destruction is seen in conditions such as ARDS or certain pneumonias.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sjögren syndrome does not primarily affect neutrophil chemotaxis. Defective neutrophil function is seen in conditions such as chronic granulomatous disease or Chédiak-Higashi syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Granulomatous inflammation with bronchial scarring is characteristic of sarcoidosis. Sjögren syndrome causes lymphocytic infiltration of exocrine glands, not granulomatous airway inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reduced alpha-1 antitrypsin is a genetic deficiency causing emphysema and liver disease, unrelated to Sjögren syndrome.

questions.stem: A pulmonologist explains to a medical student that in non-CF bronchiectasis, the organism most commonly cultured during acute exacerbations is different from that in CF patients. Which of the following organisms is most commonly isolated in acute exacerbations of non-CF bronchiectasis?

explanation.summary: In non-CF bronchiectasis, Haemophilus influenzae is the most prevalent organism found during acute exacerbations, whereas Pseudomonas aeruginosa is more common in CF-related bronchiectasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: H. influenzae is the most commonly isolated pathogen in acute exacerbations of non-CF bronchiectasis. This gram-negative coccobacillus colonizes damaged airways and contributes to the cycle of infection and inflammation. In contrast, Pseudomonas predominates in CF-related bronchiectasis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspergillus fumigatus is associated with allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis in patients with asthma or CF, but it is not the most common cause of acute bacterial exacerbations in non-CF bronchiectasis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mycobacterium tuberculosis can cause bronchiectasis as a sequela of prior infection but is not the most common pathogen isolated during acute exacerbations of non-CF bronchiectasis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common organism in CF-related bronchiectasis exacerbations but is less prevalent than H. influenzae in non-CF bronchiectasis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Staphylococcus aureus is commonly associated with early childhood respiratory infections in CF and can cause bronchiectasis exacerbations but is less common than H. influenzae in non-CF patients.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with COPD and bronchiectasis has a CT scan that shows traction bronchiectasis in the right lower lobe with associated fibrotic changes. His physician explains that this is different from true bronchiectasis. Which of the following best distinguishes traction bronchiectasis from true bronchiectasis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Traction bronchiectasis is caused by fibrosis pulling on airways, without mucopurulent sputum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Traction bronchiectasis is caused by Pseudomonas infection of the airways

choices.label: C
choices.text: Traction bronchiectasis predominantly affects the upper lobes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Traction bronchiectasis produces more sputum than true bronchiectasis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Traction bronchiectasis results from ciliary dyskinesia

explanation.summary: Traction bronchiectasis is a radiologic finding where lung parenchymal fibrosis and scarring mechanically pull on and dilate the airways, mimicking the appearance of true bronchiectasis on imaging but without the characteristic mucopurulent sputum or chronic infection cycle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Traction bronchiectasis occurs when fibrotic lung tissue retracts and pulls on the adjacent airways, causing them to appear dilated on imaging. Unlike true bronchiectasis, it is not accompanied by the typical clinical features of chronic productive cough with mucopurulent sputum, because the airway dilation is a mechanical consequence of fibrosis rather than primary airway disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Traction bronchiectasis is caused by parenchymal fibrosis, not by Pseudomonas infection. Pseudomonas is associated with true bronchiectasis exacerbations, particularly in CF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Traction bronchiectasis can occur in any lobe where fibrotic changes are present and does not have a predilection for the upper lobes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Traction bronchiectasis produces less sputum than true bronchiectasis because the characteristic mucopurulent sputum production is absent, as the airway dilation is secondary to external fibrotic traction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ciliary dyskinesia causes primary ciliary dyskinesia and Kartagener syndrome, which lead to true bronchiectasis, not traction bronchiectasis.

questions.stem: A 39-year-old woman presents with chronic sinusitis, recurrent lower respiratory infections, and bronchiectasis. Laboratory evaluation reveals markedly low serum immunoglobulin levels across all classes. Which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) is characterized by panhypogammaglobulinemia and presents with recurrent sinopulmonary infections that can lead to bronchiectasis due to inadequate humoral immune defense.
explanation.whyCorrect: CVID is the most common clinically significant primary immunodeficiency in adults. It presents with markedly reduced immunoglobulin levels across multiple classes (IgG, IgA, and often IgM), leading to recurrent sinopulmonary infections. Without adequate humoral immunity, repeated infections damage the airways and cause bronchiectasis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ABPA causes bronchiectasis through a hypersensitivity reaction to Aspergillus and is associated with elevated IgE levels, not panhypogammaglobulinemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cystic fibrosis causes bronchiectasis through defective chloride transport and thickened mucus, not through reduced immunoglobulin levels.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HIV infection reduces CD4 T cells and can increase susceptibility to infections, but it does not primarily cause panhypogammaglobulinemia. HIV-related immunodeficiency is cellular, not humoral.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Selective IgA deficiency involves low IgA only, with normal IgG and IgM levels. This patient has markedly low levels across all immunoglobulin classes, consistent with CVID rather than selective IgA deficiency.

questions.stem: A 2-day-old neonate born at home without prenatal care has not passed meconium and has bilious vomiting. Abdominal X-ray shows dilated loops of bowel with a ground-glass appearance in the right lower quadrant. The mother is of Northern European descent. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Meconium ileus, a bowel obstruction caused by thick, sticky meconium, is the presenting manifestation of cystic fibrosis in approximately 15-20% of affected neonates. The ground-glass appearance on X-ray is characteristic of inspissated meconium.
explanation.whyCorrect: In cystic fibrosis, defective CFTR chloride channels produce thick, dehydrated intestinal secretions. This causes the meconium to become abnormally thick and sticky, obstructing the terminal ileum. The ground-glass appearance on X-ray represents the inspissated meconium mixed with air, and the Northern European descent increases the pretest probability of CF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Duodenal atresia presents with a classic double-bubble sign on X-ray due to a dilated proximal duodenum and stomach, and is associated with Down syndrome, not the ground-glass pattern seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hirschsprung disease causes functional bowel obstruction from absent ganglion cells and typically shows a transition zone on contrast enema. It does not produce a ground-glass appearance on X-ray.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intussusception typically presents in infants aged 6-36 months with intermittent colicky abdominal pain, currant jelly stools, and a target sign on ultrasound, not in a 2-day-old neonate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pyloric stenosis presents at 2-8 weeks of age with nonbilious projectile vomiting, not in the first days of life with bilious vomiting and failure to pass meconium.

questions.stem: A couple who are both carriers of a CFTR gene mutation are planning to have a child. What is the probability that their child will have cystic fibrosis?

explanation.summary: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive disorder. When both parents are carriers (heterozygous), there is a 25% chance with each pregnancy that the child will inherit two mutant alleles and have CF.
explanation.whyCorrect: Since CF follows autosomal recessive inheritance, both parents must contribute a mutant CFTR allele for the child to be affected. Using a Punnett square for two heterozygous carriers (Cc x Cc), the possible offspring genotypes are CC (25%), Cc (50%), and cc (25%). Only the homozygous mutant (cc) genotype expresses the disease, giving a 25% probability.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A 0% probability would only occur if at least one parent had two normal CFTR alleles (homozygous normal), which is not the case when both parents are carriers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50% represents the probability that the child will be a carrier (heterozygous), not that the child will have the disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 75% represents the combined probability of being either a carrier or affected (Cc + cc), not the probability of having CF specifically.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 100% would require both parents to be homozygous for the CFTR mutation (both having CF), which is not the case with carrier parents.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant is evaluated for failure to thrive and recurrent respiratory infections. Newborn screening was positive for cystic fibrosis. The confirmatory diagnostic test reveals a chloride concentration of 72 mEq/L. Which of the following best explains why this test is elevated in cystic fibrosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Defective CFTR fails to reabsorb chloride from sweat in the sweat duct

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excess aldosterone stimulates chloride secretion by sweat glands

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased sodium-potassium ATPase activity in sweat glands

choices.label: D
choices.text: Overactive chloride secretion into the sweat gland lumen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced aquaporin channels prevent water reabsorption from sweat

explanation.summary: In the sweat ducts, CFTR normally reabsorbs chloride (and indirectly sodium) from primary sweat. When CFTR is defective, chloride cannot be reabsorbed, resulting in salty sweat with chloride concentrations exceeding 60 mEq/L.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unlike most epithelial surfaces where CFTR secretes chloride out of the cell, in sweat ducts CFTR absorbs chloride from the sweat back into the epithelial cells. When CFTR is dysfunctional, chloride remains in the sweat duct lumen, and sodium follows (since its reabsorption is coupled to chloride). This produces the characteristically salty sweat with elevated chloride levels used diagnostically in CF.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aldosterone regulates sodium and potassium balance primarily in the kidney. The elevated sweat chloride in CF is due to defective CFTR chloride reabsorption, not aldosterone-mediated secretion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sodium-potassium ATPase is a basolateral pump that maintains the cellular ion gradient. The elevated sweat chloride in CF is specifically due to failure of CFTR-mediated chloride reabsorption from the ductal lumen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The primary sweat produced by the sweat gland coil has normal chloride content. The problem in CF is failure to reabsorb chloride in the sweat duct, not overproduction of chloride in the primary sweat.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aquaporin dysfunction is not the mechanism of elevated sweat chloride in CF. The primary defect is in the CFTR chloride channel, and water follows the ion concentration gradient.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man with cystic fibrosis is evaluated for infertility. Semen analysis reveals azoospermia. Physical examination of the scrotum shows normal-sized testes bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his infertility?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens

explanation.summary: Up to 98% of males with cystic fibrosis are infertile due to congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (CBAVD). CFTR plays a critical role in the development of the vas deferens, and its dysfunction leads to its aplasia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Male infertility in CF is primarily caused by congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens. The CFTR protein is essential for normal development of the Wolffian duct derivatives, including the vas deferens. Without functioning CFTR, the vas deferens fails to develop, preventing sperm transport despite normal spermatogenesis in the testes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bilateral cryptorchidism would present with nonpalpable or inguinal testes on examination. This patient has normal-sized testes in the scrotum.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism would present with low testosterone, small testes, and absent secondary sexual characteristics, which are not described in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) presents with tall stature, gynecomastia, small firm testes, and elevated gonadotropins, not with normal-sized testes in a patient with known CF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Varicocele would present with a palpable scrotal mass described as a bag of worms and would cause oligospermia rather than complete azoospermia in most cases.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis presents with greasy, foul-smelling stools and poor weight gain despite a good appetite. Laboratory studies show low serum levels of vitamins A, D, E, and K. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism of his symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bile salt deficiency from cholestatic liver disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pancreatic duct obstruction causing exocrine insufficiency

explanation.summary: In CF, thick mucus obstructs the pancreatic ducts, preventing release of pancreatic lipase and other digestive enzymes into the duodenum. This causes maldigestion and malabsorption of fats and fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), resulting in steatorrhea and failure to thrive.
explanation.whyCorrect: The thick, viscous secretions in CF obstruct the pancreatic ducts, leading to pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. Without adequate pancreatic lipase, fats cannot be digested and absorbed, resulting in steatorrhea (greasy stools). Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) require fat absorption for their own uptake, so their levels become deficient as well.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While CF can cause cholestatic liver disease, the primary mechanism of fat malabsorption in CF is pancreatic duct obstruction with exocrine insufficiency, not bile salt deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Celiac disease causes villous atrophy in the small intestine from gluten sensitivity. It is a separate condition from CF and is diagnosed by anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies and duodenal biopsy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lactase deficiency causes osmotic diarrhea with watery stools after dairy consumption, not the greasy stools and fat-soluble vitamin deficiency seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth can cause malabsorption but typically presents with bloating and watery diarrhea. It is not the primary cause of malabsorption in a patient with known CF.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying the molecular biology of the CFTR protein. The most common mutation causing cystic fibrosis results in a protein that is synthesized but fails to reach the cell membrane. Which of the following best describes the cellular fate of this mutant protein?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Degraded in lysosomes after endocytosis from the cell surface

choices.label: B
choices.text: Incorporated into the cell membrane but with a nonfunctional ion channel

choices.label: C
choices.text: Retained in the rough endoplasmic reticulum due to misfolding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secreted extracellularly as an inactive soluble protein

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transported to the Golgi but improperly glycosylated

explanation.summary: The most common CFTR mutation (ΔF508) causes deletion of phenylalanine at position 508, resulting in protein misfolding. The misfolded protein is recognized by quality control mechanisms and retained in the rough endoplasmic reticulum rather than being trafficked to the cell membrane.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ΔF508 mutation, present in approximately 90% of CF patients, produces a CFTR protein that misfolds after translation. The endoplasmic reticulum has quality control systems that detect misfolded proteins and prevent their export. The misfolded CFTR is retained in the rough ER and eventually targeted for proteasomal degradation, never reaching the apical cell membrane.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lysosomal degradation after endocytosis would require the protein to first reach the cell surface, which does not occur with the ΔF508 mutation because the protein is retained in the ER.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This describes a different class of CFTR mutations (functional mutations) where the protein reaches the membrane but the ion channel does not function properly. The ΔF508 protein never reaches the membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CFTR is a transmembrane protein, not a secreted protein. Even with the mutation, it is not secreted extracellularly but rather retained intracellularly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The misfolded ΔF508 CFTR is retained in the ER and does not advance to the Golgi apparatus for glycosylation processing.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis develops polyuria, polydipsia, and unintentional weight loss. Her fasting glucose is 210 mg/dL and hemoglobin A1c is 8.2%. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of her diabetes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin resistance from chronic corticosteroid use

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mucus obstruction causing pancreatic fibrosis and impaired insulin secretion

explanation.summary: Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (CFRD) results from progressive destruction of pancreatic tissue due to mucus obstruction of pancreatic ducts, leading to fibrosis and loss of both exocrine and endocrine (islet cell) function with impaired insulin secretion.
explanation.whyCorrect: In CF, thick mucus obstructs the pancreatic ducts, causing chronic inflammation, autodigestion, and progressive replacement of pancreatic tissue with fibrosis and adipose tissue. This destroys both exocrine acinar cells and endocrine islet cells, reducing insulin production. CFRD shares features of both type 1 (reduced insulin secretion) and type 2 (some insulin resistance) diabetes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autoimmune beta cell destruction is the mechanism of type 1 diabetes mellitus, mediated by autoantibodies. CFRD is caused by structural pancreatic damage from mucus obstruction and fibrosis, not autoimmune destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While some CF patients receive corticosteroids, this patient's diabetes is most likely CFRD from chronic pancreatic damage rather than steroid-induced hyperglycemia, given her known CF and the progressive nature of her symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CF patients are typically underweight due to malabsorption, not obese. Peripheral insulin resistance from obesity is the hallmark of type 2 diabetes, not CFRD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acute viral pancreatitis is a rare cause of transient hyperglycemia. This patient's diabetes is a chronic complication of CF-related pancreatic fibrosis, not an acute infectious process.

questions.stem: A newborn screening blood test for cystic fibrosis is positive in a 3-day-old infant. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?

explanation.summary: After a positive newborn screening test for CF, the sweat chloride test (quantitative pilocarpine iontophoresis sweat test) is the gold standard confirmatory test. A sweat chloride concentration greater than 60 mEq/L confirms the diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The sweat chloride test is the diagnostic standard for confirming cystic fibrosis. Pilocarpine stimulates local sweat production, and the collected sweat is analyzed for chloride content. In CF, defective CFTR fails to reabsorb chloride from sweat, resulting in elevated sweat chloride (>60 mEq/L). This test directly assesses CFTR function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chest X-ray may show findings of bronchiectasis or recurrent infections in established CF but is not used for confirmatory diagnosis, especially in a neonate who may have a normal chest X-ray.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fecal elastase assesses pancreatic exocrine function and is low in pancreatic insufficiency. While helpful in managing CF, it is not the confirmatory diagnostic test.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nasal potential difference measurement is a research tool used to assess CFTR function in the nasal epithelium. It is not the standard confirmatory test in clinical practice.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Serum immunoglobulin levels assess humoral immune function and would be relevant for diagnosing immunodeficiency, not cystic fibrosis.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis has sputum cultures that are chronically positive for Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Which of the following properties of this organism best explains its ability to persistently colonize CF airways?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Formation of biofilms using a mucoid polysaccharide capsule

choices.label: C
choices.text: Production of IgA protease to evade mucosal immunity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid mutation rate allowing antibiotic resistance only

choices.label: E
choices.text: Spore formation allowing survival in dry environments

explanation.summary: Pseudomonas aeruginosa establishes chronic colonization in CF airways by producing a mucoid polysaccharide capsule (alginate) that forms biofilms. These biofilms protect the bacteria from host immune responses and antibiotics, making eradication extremely difficult.
explanation.whyCorrect: P. aeruginosa undergoes a phenotypic switch to a mucoid form in CF airways, producing an alginate polysaccharide capsule that enables biofilm formation. These biofilms are structured bacterial communities embedded in an extracellular matrix that shields the bacteria from phagocytosis, antibodies, complement, and antibiotic penetration, contributing to chronic pneumonia in CF.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pseudomonas is primarily an extracellular pathogen. Intracellular survival within macrophages is characteristic of organisms like Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Legionella.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While IgA protease production is a virulence factor of some respiratory pathogens like H. influenzae and S. pneumoniae, the primary mechanism of persistent Pseudomonas colonization in CF is biofilm formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While Pseudomonas does develop antibiotic resistance, this alone does not explain persistent colonization. The biofilm provides the critical structural protection that allows the organism to evade host defenses.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pseudomonas aeruginosa does not form spores. Spore formation is a characteristic of Bacillus and Clostridium species.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis undergoes spirometry. Which of the following patterns is most consistent with his underlying pulmonary disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased FEV1 and decreased FEV1/FVC ratio

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased FVC with normal FEV1/FVC ratio

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased TLC with increased FEV1/FVC ratio

explanation.summary: Cystic fibrosis causes obstructive pulmonary disease due to thick mucus plugging and airway inflammation. Spirometry shows a decreased FEV1 and a reduced FEV1/FVC ratio, which is the hallmark of obstructive lung disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: CF is classified as an obstructive pulmonary disease because the thick mucus and chronic airway inflammation obstruct airflow, particularly during expiration. Spirometry characteristically demonstrates a reduced FEV1 (reflecting impaired expiratory airflow) and a decreased FEV1/FVC ratio (below 0.70), the defining feature of an obstructive pattern.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A decreased FVC with a normal FEV1/FVC ratio describes a restrictive pattern seen in interstitial lung disease or chest wall disorders, not the obstructive pattern of CF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased FEV1 and FVC would indicate normal or supranormal lung function. CF causes decreased, not increased, expiratory flow rates.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While TLC may be increased in obstructive disease due to air trapping, the FEV1/FVC ratio would be decreased, not increased.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A decreased TLC with normal FEV1 suggests a restrictive process. In CF, TLC is normal or elevated due to air trapping, and FEV1 is reduced.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with cystic fibrosis develops progressive jaundice, hepatomegaly, and ascites. Liver biopsy shows portal fibrosis with bile duct proliferation. Which of the following best explains the pathogenesis of his liver disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune hepatitis from dysregulated T-cell response

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced bile flow from thickened biliary secretions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Viral hepatitis from contaminated blood products

explanation.summary: CF-related liver disease (focal biliary cirrhosis) results from defective CFTR in biliary epithelial cells, causing thickened, dehydrated bile that obstructs intrahepatic bile ducts (cholestasis), leading to periportal inflammation, fibrosis, and eventually biliary cirrhosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In CF, the defective CFTR protein in biliary epithelial cells (cholangiocytes) reduces chloride and bicarbonate secretion into bile. This produces thick, inspissated bile that obstructs the intrahepatic bile ducts. The resulting cholestasis triggers periportal inflammation and progressive fibrosis, eventually leading to focal biliary cirrhosis with portal hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autoimmune hepatitis involves immune-mediated hepatocellular destruction with elevated autoantibodies (ANA, anti-smooth muscle). CF liver disease is caused by mechanical bile duct obstruction, not autoimmune destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemochromatosis results from excess iron absorption and deposition. CF does not cause increased iron absorption, and the biopsy findings of bile duct proliferation with portal fibrosis are consistent with cholestatic, not iron-overload, liver disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While CF patients may have malnutrition, the biopsy shows portal fibrosis with bile duct proliferation, which is characteristic of biliary obstruction, not hepatic steatosis from malnutrition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Viral hepatitis would show hepatocellular inflammation with or without necrosis on biopsy, not the portal fibrosis with bile duct proliferation characteristic of CF-related biliary cirrhosis.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis presents with chronic nasal congestion, facial pain, and hyponasal speech. Nasal endoscopy reveals bilateral grayish, translucent masses arising from the middle meatus. Which of the following is the most likely finding?

explanation.summary: Nasal polyps are a common complication of chronic rhinosinusitis in cystic fibrosis. They appear as bilateral, grayish, translucent masses arising from the middle meatus and can cause nasal obstruction, facial pain, and hyponasal speech.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic rhinosinusitis in CF leads to persistent inflammation of the sinonasal mucosa, which can produce nasal polyps. These appear as bilateral, grayish, pedunculated, translucent masses in the middle meatus. Nasal polyps occur in approximately 10-30% of CF patients, and in a young child presenting with bilateral polyps, CF should always be considered.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Angiofibromas are vascular tumors that occur almost exclusively in adolescent males and present as a unilateral, vascular nasal mass with epistaxis, not bilateral translucent masses in a 7-year-old girl.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Inverted papillomas are unilateral benign neoplasms of the lateral nasal wall that occur in older adults, not bilateral translucent masses in a child with CF.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Septal perforation presents as a hole in the nasal septum, often causing epistaxis and a whistling sound during breathing, not bilateral masses from the middle meatus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Turbinate hypertrophy involves enlargement of the inferior or middle turbinates and appears as swollen, pink-red mucosal tissue, not discrete grayish translucent masses.

questions.stem: A molecular biology student is reviewing CFTR mutations. She learns that one class of CFTR mutations produces a protein that reaches the cell membrane but fails to conduct chloride ions normally. Which of the following best categorizes this type of mutation?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nonsense mutation causing premature stop codon

explanation.summary: Functional (gating) mutations in CFTR produce a protein that is properly synthesized, folded, and trafficked to the cell membrane, but the ion channel does not open properly or does not conduct chloride ions normally, resulting in reduced chloride transport.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gating mutations represent a class of CFTR mutations where the protein reaches the apical cell membrane but has a defective chloride channel that fails to open or conduct ions properly. Examples include the G551D mutation, which impairs ATP-dependent channel gating, reducing chloride transport despite normal protein localization.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Defective protein processing mutations (such as ΔF508) cause misfolding and retention in the endoplasmic reticulum. The protein never reaches the cell membrane, which differs from the scenario described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A nonsense mutation causes a premature stop codon, resulting in a truncated nonfunctional protein that is rapidly degraded. This would produce absent CFTR, not a membrane-localized but nonfunctional protein.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A promoter mutation would reduce transcription of the CFTR gene, decreasing the total amount of mRNA and ultimately the amount of protein synthesized, but any protein made would be structurally normal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A splicing mutation prevents proper mRNA processing, leading to abnormal transcripts and absent or severely reduced functional protein production, not a protein that reaches the membrane.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis has recurrent respiratory infections. His mother asks which organisms are most likely causing his pneumonias at his current age. Which of the following pairs best represents the typical respiratory pathogens in CF by age group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Childhood: E. coli; adulthood: Klebsiella pneumoniae

choices.label: C
choices.text: Childhood: S. aureus and H. influenzae; adulthood: P. aeruginosa

choices.label: D
choices.text: Childhood: Streptococcus agalactiae; adulthood: S. aureus

explanation.summary: In CF, childhood respiratory infections are predominantly caused by Staphylococcus aureus and Haemophilus influenzae. As patients age into adolescence and adulthood, Pseudomonas aeruginosa becomes the dominant colonizer and cause of respiratory infections.
explanation.whyCorrect: The microbiology of CF lung infections follows a predictable age-related pattern. In early childhood, S. aureus and H. influenzae are the primary pathogens. With increasing age, the mucoid form of P. aeruginosa colonizes the airways and becomes the predominant pathogen in adolescents and adults, contributing to progressive lung disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: E. coli is primarily associated with urinary tract and intra-abdominal infections, and Klebsiella is associated with hospital-acquired pneumonia in debilitated patients, not the typical CF respiratory pathogen profile.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mycoplasma causes atypical community-acquired pneumonia, and Legionella causes pneumonia from contaminated water sources. Neither is a characteristic CF respiratory pathogen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: S. agalactiae (Group B Streptococcus) is associated with neonatal sepsis and meningitis, not childhood CF infections.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pseudomonas colonization predominates in adolescents and adults, not in childhood. While Aspergillus can cause ABPA in CF, it is not the primary pathogen in adult CF lung disease.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis develops easy bruising and prolonged bleeding after a dental extraction. Laboratory studies show a prolonged prothrombin time (PT) with a normal partial thromboplastin time (PTT). Which of the following is the most likely cause of her coagulopathy?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vitamin K deficiency from fat malabsorption

explanation.summary: Pancreatic insufficiency in CF causes fat malabsorption, including malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamin K. Vitamin K is essential for the synthesis of coagulation factors II, VII, IX, and X. Deficiency causes prolonged PT because factor VII (extrinsic pathway) has the shortest half-life and is affected first.
explanation.whyCorrect: In CF, pancreatic duct obstruction leads to exocrine insufficiency and fat malabsorption, including impaired absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K). Vitamin K is required for gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. Because factor VII has the shortest half-life, the PT (which measures the extrinsic pathway) is prolonged first, while the PTT may initially remain normal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: DIC would cause prolongation of both PT and PTT, along with thrombocytopenia, elevated D-dimer, and decreased fibrinogen. This patient has an isolated prolonged PT.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemophilia A is an X-linked disorder causing factor VIII deficiency, which prolongs the PTT (intrinsic pathway), not the PT. Additionally, it occurs predominantly in males.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While CF can cause biliary cirrhosis and liver failure could prolong PT, the most common cause in a CF patient with isolated PT prolongation is vitamin K deficiency from malabsorption rather than advanced liver disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism would cause a low platelet count and prolonged bleeding time but would not cause an isolated prolonged PT with a normal PTT.

questions.stem: A geneticist explains to medical students that the CFTR gene is located on a specific chromosome. On which chromosome is the CFTR gene located?

explanation.summary: The CFTR gene is located on the long arm (q) of chromosome 7. Mutations in this gene cause defective chloride transport in epithelial cells, leading to cystic fibrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene is located at 7q31.2 on the long arm of chromosome 7. More than 2000 variants have been identified in this gene, with approximately 300 considered pathogenic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chromosome 4 harbors the gene for Huntington disease (HTT gene) but not the CFTR gene.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chromosome 11 contains the beta-globin gene cluster associated with sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia, not the CFTR gene.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chromosome 15 is associated with Prader-Willi syndrome and Angelman syndrome through imprinting defects, not with cystic fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chromosome 22 is associated with DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion) and the Philadelphia chromosome in CML, not with the CFTR gene.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis is brought to the emergency department with fever, cough, and tachypnea. His mother reports recurrent pneumonias over the past year. Chest X-ray shows a new right lower lobe infiltrate. CT scan also reveals areas of permanent bronchial dilation. Which of the following best explains why CF patients are prone to developing bronchiectasis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic airway inflammation destroying bronchial wall cartilage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excessive surfactant production causing alveolar distension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary artery hypertension dilating bronchial arteries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Recurrent pneumothoraces causing traction on airways

explanation.summary: In CF, the thick mucus promotes chronic bacterial infection and intense neutrophilic inflammation. Neutrophil-derived proteases (such as elastase) destroy the cartilage and structural proteins of the bronchial walls, causing irreversible dilation and bronchiectasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: CF is the leading cause of bronchiectasis. The thick, viscous mucus impairs mucociliary clearance, creating a favorable environment for chronic bacterial colonization. This triggers intense neutrophilic inflammation, and neutrophil-derived proteinases (elastase, matrix metalloproteinases) digest the structural proteins of the bronchial walls, destroying the cartilage and causing permanent, irreversible dilation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Congenital absence of bronchial cartilage (Williams-Campbell syndrome) is a rare developmental disorder. In CF, the cartilage is present at birth but is progressively destroyed by chronic inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surfactant is produced by type II alveolar cells and maintains alveolar stability. CF does not cause excessive surfactant production, and alveolar distension is not the mechanism of bronchiectasis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary hypertension may develop as a complication of advanced CF lung disease, but it does not cause bronchial dilation. The bronchial and pulmonary arterial systems are distinct.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While pneumothorax can occur in CF, recurrent pneumothoraces do not cause traction bronchiectasis. Traction bronchiectasis results from parenchymal fibrosis pulling on airways.

questions.stem: A physician is explaining to a patient's family how defective CFTR leads to thick mucus in the lungs. In normal lung epithelium, CFTR-mediated chloride secretion causes water to remain in the airway lumen. Which of the following best describes the immediate consequence of reduced CFTR-mediated chloride secretion in CF?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased extracellular osmolality causing water to enter the cell

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased bicarbonate secretion alkalinizing the airway surface

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased sodium secretion into the airway lumen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced mucin gene expression in goblet cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stimulation of aquaporin channels increasing luminal water

explanation.summary: When CFTR is defective, reduced chloride secretion into the airway lumen decreases extracellular ion concentration and osmolality. The lower extracellular osmolality draws water from the airway lumen into the epithelial cells, dehydrating the mucus and making it thick and viscous.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normally, CFTR secretes chloride into the airway lumen, and sodium follows down the electrochemical gradient, increasing extracellular osmolality and retaining water in the mucus layer. When CFTR is defective, less chloride enters the lumen, lowering its osmolality. Water then moves osmotically from the lumen into the cells, dehydrating the airway surface liquid and producing the characteristic thick, viscous mucus of CF.

whyWrongByChoice.B: CFTR also transports bicarbonate, but in CF, bicarbonate secretion is decreased, not increased. Reduced bicarbonate contributes to acidification and further mucus abnormalities.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sodium secretion does not increase. In fact, reduced chloride in the lumen decreases the electrical gradient for sodium movement into the lumen, contributing to decreased extracellular osmolality.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mucin gene expression in goblet cells is not directly regulated by CFTR chloride secretion. The problem in CF is the dehydration of existing mucus, not reduced mucin production.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aquaporin channels facilitate water movement along osmotic gradients. In CF, the reduced extracellular osmolality drives water into cells, away from the lumen, not toward it.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis and her partner are planning a family. She has been told she may have difficulty conceiving. Which of the following is the primary mechanism of reduced fertility in women with CF?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Premature ovarian failure from chemotherapy exposure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thickened cervical mucus impeding sperm penetration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Uterine fibroids obstructing the endometrial cavity

explanation.summary: Women with CF are usually fertile but may have difficulty conceiving because defective CFTR produces thickened cervical mucus that impedes sperm transport through the cervix. Additionally, malnutrition can cause irregular ovulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In women with CF, the same defective chloride transport that thickens airway and pancreatic secretions also affects the cervical glands. The resulting thick cervical mucus creates a physical barrier that impedes sperm penetration and transport, reducing the chances of natural conception. Most women with CF are still fertile and can conceive with assisted reproductive techniques.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Absence of the fallopian tubes is not associated with CF. Congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens is the male-specific reproductive defect in CF.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Autoimmune destruction of ovarian follicles causes premature ovarian failure and is not related to CF. CF affects epithelial chloride transport, not ovarian autoimmunity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Premature ovarian failure from chemotherapy is iatrogenic and unrelated to CF. While CF patients may receive multiple medications, chemotherapy-induced ovarian failure is not a typical CF complication.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Uterine fibroids are common benign tumors unrelated to CFTR dysfunction. They are not a recognized complication of cystic fibrosis.

questions.stem: A pediatrician is counseling new parents about newborn screening. They ask how cystic fibrosis was traditionally screened for before DNA testing became available. Which of the following serum markers was historically used for CF newborn screening?

explanation.summary: Immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) is a pancreatic enzyme precursor that was historically used for newborn CF screening. In neonates with CF, pancreatic duct obstruction causes elevated serum IRT levels. This test has largely been replaced by CFTR DNA mutation panels due to the high false-positive rate of IRT.
explanation.whyCorrect: IRT is released from the pancreas and is elevated in the blood of neonates with CF because thick mucus obstructs pancreatic ducts, causing backflow of pancreatic enzymes into the blood. While IRT was the initial screening test for CF in newborns, its high false-positive rate led to its replacement by more specific CFTR gene mutation panels in most screening programs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha-fetoprotein is used in prenatal screening for neural tube defects and Down syndrome and in monitoring hepatocellular carcinoma, not for CF screening.
whyWrongByChoice.B: C-reactive protein is a nonspecific marker of systemic inflammation used to monitor infections and inflammatory diseases. It is not used for CF screening.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fecal calprotectin is a marker of intestinal inflammation used to screen for inflammatory bowel disease. It is not used for cystic fibrosis screening.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Serum amylase may be elevated in acute pancreatitis but is not used as a newborn screening test for cystic fibrosis.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis develops worsening bone pain and bowed legs. Laboratory studies reveal low serum calcium, low serum phosphorus, and elevated alkaline phosphatase. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her bone disease?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Growth hormone deficiency from pituitary dysfunction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vitamin C deficiency causing impaired collagen synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vitamin D deficiency from fat malabsorption

explanation.summary: In CF, pancreatic insufficiency causes fat malabsorption, including malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamin D. Vitamin D deficiency impairs calcium absorption and bone mineralization, leading to rickets in children (bone pain, bowed legs, low calcium and phosphorus, elevated alkaline phosphatase).
explanation.whyCorrect: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin that requires pancreatic lipase-mediated fat digestion for absorption. In CF, pancreatic insufficiency causes fat malabsorption and vitamin D deficiency. Without adequate vitamin D, intestinal calcium absorption is impaired, leading to hypocalcemia and rickets in children, characterized by defective bone mineralization, bone pain, and skeletal deformities.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Copper deficiency can cause osteoporosis and bone abnormalities but typically presents with neutropenia and anemia. It is not a characteristic complication of CF-related malabsorption.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Growth hormone deficiency causes short stature and delayed bone age but does not cause the laboratory pattern of low calcium, low phosphorus, and elevated alkaline phosphatase seen in rickets.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Osteogenesis imperfecta is a genetic collagen disorder causing brittle bones and blue sclerae. It is unrelated to CF and does not cause the metabolic bone disease laboratory pattern described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) causes impaired collagen synthesis leading to bleeding gums, petechiae, and poor wound healing, not rickets with abnormal calcium and phosphorus metabolism.
