questions.stem: A medical student is reviewing the epidemiology of cystic fibrosis. She learns that the disease has a significantly higher incidence in one particular population group compared to others. Which of the following populations has the highest prevalence of cystic fibrosis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hispanic populations of Central American descent

explanation.summary: Cystic fibrosis is most prevalent in white populations of European ancestry, with a 3- to 10-fold increase in incidence compared to other population groups. The estimated incidence in this group is approximately 1 in 2,000 to 4,000 live births.
explanation.whyCorrect: CF is most common in individuals of European descent, with the highest carrier frequency (approximately 1 in 25) in Northern European populations. The high prevalence may be related to a heterozygote advantage against certain infections, similar to the relationship between sickle cell trait and malaria resistance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CF is rare in East Asian populations, with an estimated incidence of approximately 1 in 30,000 to 1 in 100,000 live births, far lower than in European-descent populations.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While CF does occur in Hispanic populations, the incidence is lower than in white populations of European ancestry.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CF is uncommon in Native American populations. The high-prevalence CFTR mutations found in European populations are much less frequent in this group.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CF is relatively rare in sub-Saharan African populations, with a significantly lower incidence compared to European-descent populations.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis homozygous for the F508del mutation is started on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Trikafta). At a follow-up visit 3 months later, his FEV1 has improved from 65% to 82% predicted, and he has gained 4 kg. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which elexacaftor and tezacaftor contribute to this improvement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase the open probability of CFTR channels at the cell surface

choices.label: B
choices.text: Improve folding and trafficking of CFTR protein to the cell membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cleave extracellular DNA in airway secretions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide exogenous chloride transport independent of CFTR

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibit epithelial sodium channel absorption to restore airway hydration

explanation.summary: Elexacaftor and tezacaftor are CFTR correctors that improve the folding, intracellular processing, and trafficking of mutant CFTR protein to the cell surface, increasing the quantity of functional channels at the membrane.
explanation.whyCorrect: CFTR correctors such as elexacaftor and tezacaftor address the protein misfolding defect caused by mutations like F508del. By improving intracellular processing and trafficking, they allow more CFTR protein to reach the apical cell membrane, where the potentiator component (ivacaftor) can then enhance channel gating.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increasing the open probability of CFTR channels at the cell surface is the mechanism of CFTR potentiators (e.g., ivacaftor), not correctors like elexacaftor and tezacaftor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cleaving extracellular DNA in airway secretions is the mechanism of dornase alfa (DNase), an airway clearance therapy, not a CFTR modulator.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CFTR modulators work by restoring function of the patient's own CFTR protein, not by providing an alternative chloride transport pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inhibiting the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) is not the mechanism of CFTR modulators. While ENaC hyperactivity contributes to airway dehydration in CF, these drugs target the CFTR protein itself.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis and chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonization asks why her pulmonologist prescribes inhaled tobramycin rather than oral antibiotics for long-term suppressive therapy. Which of the following is the primary advantage of the inhaled route in this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhaled antibiotics eradicate Pseudomonas biofilms more completely

choices.label: B
choices.text: High airway drug concentrations are achieved while minimizing systemic toxicity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhaled tobramycin does not promote antibiotic resistance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tobramycin is inactivated in the gastrointestinal tract and cannot be given orally

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhaled antibiotics have a broader antimicrobial spectrum than systemic agents

explanation.summary: Inhaled antibiotics are preferred for chronic Pseudomonas suppression in CF because they maximize drug concentration at the site of infection (the airways) while minimizing systemic absorption and associated toxicities such as nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inhaled tobramycin delivers high local concentrations directly to the airways, which is critical for treating endobronchial Pseudomonas infection. This route significantly reduces systemic exposure, thereby limiting the nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity that are well-known risks of aminoglycosides.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While higher local concentrations may help penetrate biofilms, inhaled antibiotics do not completely eradicate Pseudomonas biofilms. The primary advantage is the favorable local-to-systemic concentration ratio.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhaled antibiotics can still promote resistance, though targeted delivery may reduce selective pressure elsewhere. Resistance prevention is not the primary advantage cited.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although oral bioavailability of aminoglycosides is indeed poor, tobramycin can be given intravenously. The question asks about the advantage of inhaled over other systemic routes, not just oral.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The antimicrobial spectrum of an antibiotic is determined by its chemical properties, not its route of administration. Inhaled tobramycin has the same spectrum as parenteral tobramycin.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with cystic fibrosis presents with polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and an unintentional 3-kg weight loss over 2 months. His hemoglobin A1c is 7.8% and fasting glucose is 180 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacologic treatment for his condition?

explanation.summary: CF-related diabetes (CFRD) results from progressive pancreatic damage leading to insulin deficiency. Because the primary defect is insufficient insulin secretion, insulin therapy is the recommended treatment. Oral hypoglycemics lack evidence of benefit in CFRD.
explanation.whyCorrect: CFRD is caused by destruction of pancreatic islet cells from thick mucus plugging and chronic inflammation, resulting in insulin deficiency. Insulin is the only recommended pharmacologic therapy because it directly addresses the deficient hormone and supports the high-calorie nutritional needs of CF patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metformin targets insulin resistance and hepatic glucose production. CFRD is primarily an insulin-deficiency state, and oral agents including metformin are not recommended due to insufficient evidence and potential GI side effects in CF patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pioglitazone is a thiazolidinedione that improves insulin sensitivity. It does not address the insulin deficiency underlying CFRD and is not recommended.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glipizide is a sulfonylurea that stimulates insulin release from beta cells. With progressive beta cell destruction in CFRD, there is insufficient functional tissue for sulfonylureas to act on effectively.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Caloric restriction is contraindicated in CF patients who typically require a high-calorie, nutrient-dense diet to maintain weight and pulmonary function. Exercise is encouraged but is not the primary treatment for CFRD.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis uses inhaled dornase alfa daily as part of her airway clearance regimen. Her mother asks how this medication works. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of dornase alfa?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Draws water into the airway lumen by osmotic effect

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cleaves extracellular DNA released by neutrophils, reducing sputum viscosity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Breaks disulfide bonds within mucus glycoproteins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stimulates ciliary beat frequency to enhance mucociliary clearance

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blocks epithelial sodium channels to increase airway surface liquid

explanation.summary: Dornase alfa is a recombinant human DNase that cleaves extracellular DNA released by degranulating neutrophils in CF airways. This reduces the viscosity of purulent sputum, facilitating airway clearance.
explanation.whyCorrect: CF airways are characterized by chronic neutrophilic inflammation. When neutrophils degrade, they release large amounts of DNA into the sputum, contributing significantly to its viscosity. Dornase alfa enzymatically cleaves this extracellular DNA, thinning secretions and improving airway clearance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Drawing water into the airway lumen via osmotic effect is the mechanism of hypertonic saline and inhaled mannitol, not dornase alfa.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Breaking disulfide bonds within mucus glycoproteins is the mechanism of acetylcysteine (NAC), a mucolytic agent distinct from dornase alfa.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dornase alfa does not directly stimulate ciliary beat frequency. It works by reducing sputum viscosity through DNA cleavage, which indirectly facilitates mucociliary clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blocking epithelial sodium channels is not the mechanism of any currently approved CF airway clearance therapy. CFTR modulators restore chloride secretion but do not directly block ENaC.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis presents with greasy, foul-smelling stools and poor weight gain despite a high-calorie diet. His physician prescribes pancrelipase (Creon) to be taken with every meal and snack. Which of the following best explains the rationale for this treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thick secretions obstruct pancreatic ducts, impairing enzyme delivery to the small intestine

choices.label: B
choices.text: CFTR dysfunction directly inhibits pancreatic enzyme synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic antibiotic use has destroyed the intestinal flora needed for fat digestion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bile acid synthesis is impaired due to CF-related liver cirrhosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Small intestinal villous atrophy reduces the absorptive surface area

explanation.summary: In CF, defective CFTR leads to thick, dehydrated secretions that obstruct pancreatic ducts, preventing digestive enzymes from reaching the small intestine. This causes pancreatic exocrine insufficiency with fat malabsorption (steatorrhea), requiring pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dysfunctional CFTR in the pancreatic ductal epithelium results in inadequate chloride and water secretion, producing thick mucus that plugs the ducts. This obstructs delivery of lipase, amylase, and protease to the duodenum, causing maldigestion and malabsorption, especially of fat.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The pancreatic acinar cells can still synthesize enzymes. The problem is that thick secretions obstruct the ducts, preventing enzyme delivery, not a primary defect in enzyme production.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While CF patients frequently use antibiotics, the steatorrhea results from pancreatic exocrine insufficiency due to ductal obstruction, not from disruption of intestinal flora.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although CF can cause liver disease and biliary cirrhosis, the primary cause of steatorrhea in CF is pancreatic exocrine insufficiency from ductal obstruction, not impaired bile acid synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Villous atrophy is characteristic of celiac disease, not cystic fibrosis. The intestinal mucosa is generally intact in CF; the malabsorption results from inadequate enzyme delivery.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis has been using chest physiotherapy with a percussion vest twice daily since childhood. Which of the following best describes how this device aids in the management of her pulmonary disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delivers aerosolized medication directly to the lower airways

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mechanically vibrates the chest to break up thick mucus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provides continuous positive airway pressure to prevent atelectasis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Strengthens the respiratory muscles through resistance training

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increases tidal volume by splinting the chest wall

explanation.summary: A percussion vest is a chest physiotherapy device that mechanically vibrates the patient's chest at scheduled sessions to break up the thick, tenacious mucus characteristic of CF, facilitating expectoration and airway clearance.
explanation.whyCorrect: The percussion vest oscillates rapidly against the chest wall, generating vibrations that loosen and mobilize thick airway secretions. This is a cornerstone of airway clearance in CF, typically performed multiple times daily.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The percussion vest does not deliver aerosolized medications. Nebulizers and metered-dose inhalers are used for drug delivery. The vest is purely a mechanical airway clearance device.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is used for conditions like sleep apnea and to maintain alveolar recruitment. The percussion vest works through oscillation, not sustained positive pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The vest does not provide resistance training for respiratory muscles. Its purpose is to dislodge mucus through external chest wall vibration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The vest does not increase tidal volume or splint the chest wall. Its therapeutic effect comes from high-frequency oscillations that mobilize secretions.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis and chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection is prescribed azithromycin three times weekly. She asks why she is taking an antibiotic even though she does not currently have an acute infection. Which of the following best explains the rationale for chronic azithromycin use in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It eradicates Pseudomonas aeruginosa from the airways

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has anti-inflammatory properties that reduce exacerbations

choices.label: C
choices.text: It provides prophylaxis against Aspergillus fumigatus colonization

choices.label: D
choices.text: It augments the activity of inhaled tobramycin

choices.label: E
choices.text: It prevents the development of CF-related diabetes

explanation.summary: Chronic azithromycin therapy in CF patients with Pseudomonas colonization is used primarily for its anti-inflammatory effects, which include modulating the neutrophilic inflammatory response and potentially disrupting bacterial biofilms. This reduces exacerbation frequency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Azithromycin's benefits in CF appear largely anti-inflammatory, with possible anti-biofilm and quorum-sensing interference effects. Clinical trials have demonstrated reduced pulmonary exacerbations in CF patients with chronic Pseudomonas infection who take azithromycin regularly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Azithromycin does not eradicate Pseudomonas, which is intrinsically resistant to macrolides. Its benefits come from immunomodulatory and anti-biofilm properties rather than direct bactericidal activity against Pseudomonas.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Azithromycin does not provide antifungal prophylaxis. It is a macrolide antibiotic with no activity against Aspergillus species.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Azithromycin is not used to augment tobramycin activity. These are independent therapies with different mechanisms and targets.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CFRD results from progressive pancreatic islet cell damage, not from airway inflammation. Azithromycin has no effect on preventing diabetes.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis is started on inhaled hypertonic saline as part of his airway clearance regimen. His physician also prescribes a short-acting bronchodilator to be used immediately before the hypertonic saline. Which of the following best explains the reason for administering the bronchodilator first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To prevent bronchospasm triggered by the hypertonic saline

choices.label: B
choices.text: To increase mucus production for more effective clearance

choices.label: C
choices.text: To enhance sodium absorption from the airway surface

choices.label: D
choices.text: To reduce the risk of hypernatremia from saline absorption

choices.label: E
choices.text: To increase the osmolality of the hypertonic saline

explanation.summary: Hypertonic saline can provoke bronchospasm in some CF patients. Pre-treatment with a short-acting bronchodilator such as albuterol improves tolerability and ensures adequate drug delivery to the distal airways.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypertonic saline is an osmotic agent that draws water into the airway lumen, improving mucus hydration and clearance. However, it can irritate the airways and trigger bronchospasm. Administering albuterol beforehand relaxes airway smooth muscle, preventing bronchospasm and improving tolerability of the nebulized hypertonic saline.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bronchodilators do not increase mucus production. They relax bronchial smooth muscle. The goal of hypertonic saline is to improve clearance of existing mucus, not to produce more.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Enhanced sodium absorption is not the goal. Hypertonic saline works by drawing water osmotically into the airway lumen to hydrate mucus, not by promoting sodium absorption.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Systemic hypernatremia from inhaled hypertonic saline is not a clinically significant concern. The bronchodilator is given to prevent local airway reactivity, not systemic electrolyte effects.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A bronchodilator does not alter the osmolality of hypertonic saline. These are separate medications with distinct mechanisms.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with cystic fibrosis is evaluated for progressive jaundice and hepatomegaly. Laboratory studies show elevated alkaline phosphatase, conjugated bilirubin, and gamma-glutamyl transferase. Liver biopsy reveals inspissated eosinophilic material within bile ductules with surrounding portal fibrosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: CF-related liver disease occurs when thick, dehydrated biliary secretions obstruct intrahepatic bile ductules. This leads to focal biliary cirrhosis, characterized by inspissated bile within ductules and surrounding portal fibrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Defective CFTR in biliary epithelial cells produces thick, dehydrated bile that obstructs small bile ductules. This causes focal areas of bile duct proliferation, portal inflammation, and progressive fibrosis, termed focal biliary cirrhosis—the characteristic hepatic lesion of CF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autoimmune hepatitis presents with markedly elevated transaminases, positive autoantibodies (ANA, anti-smooth muscle), and interface hepatitis on biopsy. The cholestatic pattern and inspissated material point to biliary obstruction, not autoimmune destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease shows hepatic steatosis (fat accumulation) on biopsy, not inspissated ductular material. While CF patients can develop steatosis, the biopsy findings described are classic for biliary cirrhosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primary sclerosing cholangitis involves inflammation and fibrosis of large bile ducts with an onion-skin periductal fibrosis pattern. CF liver disease primarily involves small intrahepatic ductules with inspissated secretions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CFTR modulators can cause transaminase elevations, but the biopsy findings of inspissated material in bile ductules with portal fibrosis are characteristic of CF-related biliary disease, not drug-induced hepatotoxicity.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis who is homozygous for the G551D mutation is started on ivacaftor monotherapy. Her sweat chloride decreases from 98 to 48 mEq/L over 2 weeks. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of ivacaftor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Corrects misfolding of CFTR protein in the endoplasmic reticulum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increases the open probability of CFTR channels already at the cell surface

choices.label: C
choices.text: Replaces defective CFTR with a functional gene via viral vector

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibits proteolytic degradation of CFTR in the proteasome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stimulates alternative chloride channels to compensate for CFTR loss

explanation.summary: Ivacaftor is a CFTR potentiator that increases the open probability of CFTR channels already present at the cell surface. The G551D mutation causes a gating defect where CFTR reaches the membrane but does not open normally, making it ideal for potentiator therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ivacaftor binds to CFTR protein at the cell surface and increases the time the channel spends in the open state, enhancing chloride transport. The G551D mutation produces a CFTR protein that traffics normally to the membrane but has impaired gating, so potentiator monotherapy alone can substantially restore function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Correcting protein misfolding in the ER is the mechanism of CFTR correctors (e.g., lumacaftor, tezacaftor, elexacaftor), not potentiators like ivacaftor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gene therapy for CF remains investigational. Ivacaftor is a small-molecule drug that enhances the function of existing mutant CFTR protein.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ivacaftor does not inhibit proteasomal degradation. CFTR correctors address trafficking and processing, while ivacaftor acts on channels that have already reached the surface.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ivacaftor acts directly on the CFTR channel itself, not on alternative chloride channels. Its therapeutic effect depends on the presence of CFTR protein at the cell surface.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man with cystic fibrosis has a pulmonary exacerbation with increased cough and purulent sputum. Sputum culture grows Pseudomonas aeruginosa. His physician initiates intravenous antipseudomonal antibiotics with two agents. Which of the following best explains the rationale for double coverage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To prevent allergic reactions to a single antibiotic

choices.label: B
choices.text: To achieve synergistic killing and mitigate the high resistance risk

choices.label: C
choices.text: To reduce the total duration of antibiotic therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: To provide coverage against anaerobic co-pathogens

choices.label: E
choices.text: To allow lower doses of each drug and prevent renal toxicity

explanation.summary: During acute pulmonary exacerbations in CF, double coverage for Pseudomonas aeruginosa is used because of the severity of infection and the high risk of antibiotic resistance in this population. Two agents provide synergistic bactericidal activity and reduce the likelihood of resistance emergence.
explanation.whyCorrect: CF patients face repeated antibiotic exposures that select for resistant Pseudomonas strains. Dual antipseudomonal therapy provides synergistic killing and reduces the probability that resistant organisms will survive treatment, improving clinical outcomes during acute exacerbations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Double coverage is not used to prevent allergic reactions. Allergy management involves drug selection and desensitization, not adding a second antibiotic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Double coverage does not necessarily reduce the total duration of therapy. Treatment duration is determined by clinical response, not by the number of antibiotics used.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anaerobic co-pathogens are not the primary concern in CF pulmonary exacerbations. The dual therapy is specifically targeted at Pseudomonas to provide synergy and prevent resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Double coverage does not typically allow lower doses; in fact, CF patients often require higher doses due to altered pharmacokinetics. The goal is synergy and resistance prevention, not dose reduction.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis has night blindness and dry, scaly skin. Her physician suspects a nutritional deficiency. Which of the following vitamins is most likely deficient, and why is this deficiency common in CF?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vitamin A, due to fat malabsorption from pancreatic insufficiency

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vitamin B12, due to terminal ileum inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vitamin C, due to increased oxidative stress in the lungs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Folate, due to impaired intestinal absorption of water-soluble vitamins

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron, due to chronic pulmonary hemorrhage

explanation.summary: Night blindness and xerosis (dry, scaly skin) are classic manifestations of vitamin A deficiency. In CF, pancreatic exocrine insufficiency causes fat malabsorption, leading to deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
explanation.whyCorrect: Vitamin A is a fat-soluble vitamin whose absorption depends on adequate pancreatic lipase activity and bile salts for micelle formation. CF patients with pancreatic insufficiency have impaired fat digestion, leading to malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins. Night blindness (impaired rhodopsin synthesis) and xerosis are hallmark signs of vitamin A deficiency.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vitamin B12 deficiency occurs with terminal ileum disease (e.g., Crohn disease) or pernicious anemia. CF does not typically cause terminal ileum inflammation, and B12 is water-soluble, not affected by pancreatic insufficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vitamin C is water-soluble and its absorption is not significantly impaired in CF. Night blindness is not a feature of vitamin C deficiency; scurvy presents with bleeding gums and poor wound healing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Folate is a water-soluble vitamin that is not typically deficient in CF. Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are the ones at risk due to fat malabsorption.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron deficiency causes microcytic anemia, not night blindness. Chronic pulmonary hemorrhage is not a typical feature of CF.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with cystic fibrosis has an FEV1 of 22% predicted despite maximal medical therapy, frequent hospitalizations for pulmonary exacerbations, and progressive exercise intolerance. He is evaluated for definitive treatment. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Addition of a second CFTR modulator regimen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Placement on continuous home mechanical ventilation

explanation.summary: For end-stage CF lung disease with severely reduced FEV1 despite maximal therapy, bilateral lung transplantation is the definitive treatment option that can significantly improve survival and quality of life.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lung transplantation is considered when CF patients have progressive decline to an FEV1 below 30% predicted despite optimal therapy, with frequent exacerbations and functional deterioration. Bilateral transplant is required because leaving a native CF lung would serve as a reservoir for chronic infection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Systemic corticosteroids are not standard therapy for CF lung disease and carry significant side effects including glucose intolerance, osteoporosis, and growth impairment. They do not alter the trajectory of end-stage disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CFTR modulators are most effective when started earlier in the disease course. At this advanced stage with severely reduced lung function, adding another modulator regimen is unlikely to provide meaningful benefit.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic mechanical ventilation does not address the underlying lung destruction and is generally considered a bridge to transplant or palliative measure, not a definitive treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Intravenous immunoglobulin has no established role in CF management. The recurrent infections in CF are due to impaired mucociliary clearance, not immunoglobulin deficiency.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis is started on elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor. At his 6-month follow-up, liver function tests reveal ALT 3 times the upper limit of normal. He is asymptomatic. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this laboratory finding?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adverse effect of CFTR modulator therapy

explanation.summary: Hepatotoxicity with transaminase elevations is a recognized adverse effect of CFTR modulator therapy, including elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor. Liver function tests should be monitored periodically in patients on these medications.
explanation.whyCorrect: CFTR modulators, including the triple combination therapy, carry a known risk of hepatotoxicity manifesting as transaminase elevations. This is typically asymptomatic and detected on routine monitoring. Dose adjustment or temporary discontinuation may be required for significant elevations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CF-related biliary cirrhosis presents with a cholestatic pattern (elevated alkaline phosphatase, GGT, conjugated bilirubin) rather than isolated transaminase elevation. The temporal association with starting CFTR modulators favors drug effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While hepatitis A is possible, the temporal relationship to initiating a new medication known to cause transaminase elevations makes drug-related hepatotoxicity far more likely in this context.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Wilson disease typically presents in older children/adolescents with additional features such as Kayser-Fleischer rings, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and low ceruloplasmin. Isolated transaminase elevation after starting CFTR modulators points to drug effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Autoimmune hepatitis would typically show positive autoantibodies (ANA, anti-smooth muscle) and a hepatocellular injury pattern, often with hypergammaglobulinemia. The timing after starting a hepatotoxic medication makes drug effect most likely.

questions.stem: A nutritionist counsels the parents of a 6-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis about his dietary needs. Despite pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy, the child remains underweight. Which of the following dietary recommendations is most appropriate for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Low-fat, high-fiber diet to reduce steatorrhea

choices.label: B
choices.text: High-calorie, high-fat diet with fat-soluble vitamin supplementation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protein-restricted diet to reduce pancreatic enzyme burden

choices.label: D
choices.text: Standard age-appropriate caloric intake with no modifications

choices.label: E
choices.text: Carbohydrate-restricted ketogenic diet to prevent CFRD

explanation.summary: CF patients require a high-calorie, high-fat diet due to malabsorption and increased metabolic demands from chronic lung disease. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K) is essential because pancreatic insufficiency impairs absorption of these vitamins.
explanation.whyCorrect: Children with CF have increased caloric needs due to chronic inflammation, increased work of breathing, and malabsorption despite enzyme replacement. A high-calorie, high-fat diet (typically 120-150% of recommended intake) with supplemental fat-soluble vitamins is the standard nutritional approach to support growth and pulmonary health.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A low-fat diet would worsen malnutrition in CF patients who already struggle to absorb adequate calories. Fat is a concentrated calorie source that these patients need, and steatorrhea is managed with enzyme replacement, not fat restriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Protein restriction is not recommended in CF. These patients need increased protein intake to support growth and combat the catabolic effects of chronic infection and inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Standard age-appropriate caloric intake is insufficient for CF patients who have significantly increased energy expenditure and ongoing malabsorption despite enzyme supplementation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A carbohydrate-restricted ketogenic diet is not recommended for CF patients. Adequate carbohydrate intake is important for energy, and dietary restriction does not prevent CFRD, which results from pancreatic islet cell damage.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis develops a pulmonary exacerbation requiring hospitalization. Sputum culture grows mucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa. She is currently on inhaled tobramycin suppressive therapy. The CF pharmacist notes that the patient may require higher-than-standard antibiotic doses. Which of the following best explains why CF patients often need higher antibiotic doses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired hepatic metabolism leads to slower drug activation

choices.label: B
choices.text: CF can alter antibiotic pharmacokinetics, increasing clearance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thick airway mucus inactivates antibiotics enzymatically

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic malnutrition reduces antibiotic receptor binding affinity

choices.label: E
choices.text: CFTR modulators induce cytochrome P450 enzymes that metabolize antibiotics

explanation.summary: CF can alter antibiotic pharmacokinetics, often increasing renal clearance and volume of distribution. This necessitates higher doses to achieve adequate serum and tissue concentrations for therapeutic efficacy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with CF frequently demonstrate augmented renal clearance and increased volume of distribution for many antibiotics, including aminoglycosides and beta-lactams. These pharmacokinetic alterations mean that standard doses may produce subtherapeutic concentrations, requiring dose increases guided by therapeutic drug monitoring.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Impaired hepatic metabolism would lead to higher drug levels, not lower. CF patients typically have enhanced, not impaired, drug clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Airway mucus does not enzymatically inactivate antibiotics. While mucus can impair drug penetration, this is addressed by inhaled routes and higher dosing, not by enzymatic degradation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Malnutrition does not reduce antibiotic receptor binding affinity. Antibiotic targets are on the bacteria, not the host, and nutrient status does not alter bacterial drug target affinity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While CFTR modulators do interact with some CYP450 enzymes, the need for higher antibiotic doses in CF predates the era of CFTR modulators and reflects intrinsic pharmacokinetic differences in CF patients.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis and mild lung disease is prescribed high-dose ibuprofen to slow the decline in lung function. Which of the following adverse effects requires periodic monitoring in this patient?

explanation.summary: High-dose NSAID therapy with ibuprofen can slow the rate of lung function decline in children with mild CF lung disease, but it requires close periodic monitoring for GI bleeding and renal impairment, which are well-known NSAID adverse effects.
explanation.whyCorrect: NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase, reducing prostaglandin-mediated gastric mucosal protection and renal vasodilation. High-dose ibuprofen used chronically increases the risk of GI hemorrhage and nephrotoxicity, necessitating regular monitoring of serum creatinine and screening for occult blood loss.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hepatic fibrosis is not a typical adverse effect of ibuprofen. While NSAIDs can rarely cause hepatotoxicity, the primary monitoring concerns with high-dose ibuprofen are GI and renal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Optic neuritis is associated with ethambutol, not ibuprofen. NSAIDs are not known to cause significant ocular toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bone marrow suppression is not a recognized adverse effect of ibuprofen at standard or high doses. This is more characteristic of certain disease-modifying drugs or chemotherapeutic agents.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary eosinophilia is not associated with NSAID use. NSAIDs can cause aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease with bronchospasm but not eosinophilic lung infiltrates.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis and CF-related diabetes is counseled about her nutritional management. Her endocrinologist emphasizes that dietary recommendations for CFRD differ from those for type 2 diabetes. Which of the following best describes the nutritional approach for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Caloric restriction to achieve a BMI less than 25 kg/m²

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low-carbohydrate, high-protein diet to minimize insulin requirements

choices.label: C
choices.text: High-calorie, nutrient-dense diet coordinated with insulin therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Very-low-fat diet to reduce pancreatic enzyme requirements

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intermittent fasting to improve insulin sensitivity

explanation.summary: Unlike type 2 diabetes, CFRD management prioritizes maintaining adequate caloric intake with a high-calorie, nutrient-dense diet because CF patients need to support weight and lung function. Insulin doses are adjusted to the diet rather than restricting calories.
explanation.whyCorrect: CF patients with CFRD still require high-calorie, nutrient-dense diets to maintain weight and pulmonary health. Insulin therapy is coordinated with dietary intake rather than using dietary restriction as the primary glucose control strategy. Management should involve CF, endocrine, and nutrition specialists.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Caloric restriction is contraindicated in CF patients who typically need 120-150% of standard caloric intake. Weight loss would worsen pulmonary outcomes and overall prognosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Low-carbohydrate diets are not recommended for CFRD. Adequate carbohydrate intake is needed for energy, and insulin is adjusted to cover dietary needs rather than restricting macronutrients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Very-low-fat diets would deprive CF patients of an essential concentrated calorie source. Fat malabsorption is managed with pancreatic enzyme replacement, not by eliminating dietary fat.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Intermittent fasting is inappropriate for CF patients who require consistent, frequent caloric intake to maintain weight and nutritional status.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old man with cystic fibrosis presents for routine genotyping to determine eligibility for CFTR modulator therapy. His genotype reveals he is homozygous for the F508del mutation. His physician recommends triple combination therapy with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor. Which of the following best explains why triple therapy rather than a single potentiator is needed for the F508del mutation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: F508del causes a nonsense mutation that produces no CFTR protein

choices.label: B
choices.text: F508del causes both a trafficking defect and a gating defect requiring correctors and a potentiator

choices.label: C
choices.text: Triple therapy is needed to overcome the gene dosage effect of homozygosity

choices.label: D
choices.text: F508del produces a CFTR protein that is resistant to potentiator binding

choices.label: E
choices.text: Triple therapy is required because F508del affects three separate exons of the CFTR gene

explanation.summary: The F508del mutation causes CFTR protein to misfold in the endoplasmic reticulum, resulting in defective trafficking to the cell surface. Even the small amount that reaches the membrane has impaired gating. Correctors restore trafficking while the potentiator improves channel opening.
explanation.whyCorrect: F508del produces a CFTR protein with multiple defects: misfolding that leads to ER retention and proteasomal degradation (addressed by correctors like elexacaftor and tezacaftor), and reduced channel open probability at the surface (addressed by the potentiator ivacaftor). Combining corrector(s) + potentiator addresses both defects simultaneously.

whyWrongByChoice.A: F508del is a missense/deletion mutation that produces a misfolded CFTR protein, not a nonsense mutation. Nonsense mutations (e.g., Class I) produce truncated or no protein and would not benefit from correctors or potentiators.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gene dosage does not determine the number of drugs needed. Both heterozygous and homozygous F508del patients benefit from triple therapy because the mutation itself causes multiple protein defects.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The F508del CFTR protein is not resistant to potentiator binding. However, a potentiator alone is insufficient because too little F508del CFTR reaches the cell surface without correctors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: F508del is a three-nucleotide deletion in a single exon (exon 11) resulting in loss of phenylalanine at position 508. The three drugs in triple therapy address different protein defects, not different exons.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis is prescribed acetylcysteine as an adjunctive mucolytic. She develops cough and wheezing shortly after inhaling the medication. Which of the following best explains this adverse effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acetylcysteine directly activates mast cell degranulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acetylcysteine can cause airway irritation and bronchospasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sulfhydryl groups generate toxic reactive oxygen species

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acetylcysteine inhibits surfactant production in the airways

explanation.summary: Acetylcysteine is a mucolytic that disrupts disulfide bonds within mucus glycoproteins, reducing viscosity. However, it can cause airway irritation and bronchospasm in some patients, which may limit its use and require pre-treatment with a bronchodilator.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inhaled acetylcysteine can irritate airway mucosa and trigger reflex bronchospasm, particularly in patients with hyperreactive airways like those with CF. This is a well-recognized adverse effect that sometimes limits its use or requires bronchodilator pre-treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acetylcysteine does not directly activate mast cells. The bronchospasm is due to direct airway irritation, not a mast cell-mediated mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The reaction is not IgE-mediated. Bronchospasm from acetylcysteine is a local irritant response, not an allergic hypersensitivity reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acetylcysteine's sulfhydryl groups actually serve as antioxidants (as a precursor to glutathione), not as generators of reactive oxygen species.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acetylcysteine does not inhibit surfactant production. Its mechanism involves breaking disulfide bonds in mucus glycoproteins, not interfering with surfactant synthesis.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis is being started on CFTR modulator therapy. His ophthalmologist recommends baseline and periodic eye examinations. Which of the following ocular adverse effects is the primary concern with CFTR modulator use in pediatric patients?

explanation.summary: CFTR modulators have been rarely associated with cataracts in pediatric patients. Baseline and periodic ophthalmologic examinations are recommended for children starting these medications.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cataracts have been reported as a rare adverse effect of CFTR modulator therapy, particularly in pediatric patients. Guidelines recommend baseline and periodic lens examinations in children and adolescents receiving these drugs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glaucoma is not a recognized adverse effect of CFTR modulator therapy. The primary ocular concern in pediatric patients is cataract formation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Retinal detachment has not been associated with CFTR modulator use. This is typically related to high myopia, trauma, or vitreous degeneration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Optic atrophy is not linked to CFTR modulators. This finding is associated with conditions such as multiple sclerosis or ethambutol toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Corneal ulceration is not a reported adverse effect of CFTR modulators. This typically results from contact lens wear, infection, or dry eye syndromes.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis is hospitalized for a severe pulmonary exacerbation. She is currently on inhaled dornase alfa, hypertonic saline, chest physiotherapy, and inhaled tobramycin. Her physician discusses the importance of infection prevention measures. Which of the following is the most important infection control practice for this hospitalized CF patient?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Strict contact and respiratory precautions with distancing from other CF patients

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continuous ultraviolet light sterilization of the hospital room

choices.label: E
choices.text: Restriction of all visitors to prevent viral transmission

explanation.summary: Hospitalized CF patients require strict contact and respiratory precautions, including physical distancing from other patients with respiratory infections, to prevent cross-infection with resistant organisms like Pseudomonas and Burkholderia species.
explanation.whyCorrect: Infections cause high mortality in CF, so prevention is critical. Strict contact and respiratory precautions are essential for hospitalized CF patients, along with physical distancing from other patients with respiratory infections, to prevent acquisition of new resistant pathogens or cross-transmission of existing ones.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Prophylactic systemic antifungals are not routinely recommended for hospitalized CF patients. Antifungal therapy is reserved for diagnosed fungal infections such as allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Empiric anti-tubercular therapy is not indicated without evidence of tuberculosis. CF patients are not at inherently increased risk for TB, and treatment should be culture-guided.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Continuous UV light sterilization is not a standard infection control measure for CF patients. Standard contact and respiratory precautions are the evidence-based approach.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Complete visitor restriction is not necessary or recommended. Appropriate hand hygiene and screening for active respiratory illness in visitors are sufficient.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with a 2-day history of sore throat, fever of 38.9°C (102°F), and painful swallowing. Physical examination reveals tonsillar enlargement with purulent exudates and tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. She has no cough, rhinorrhea, or conjunctivitis. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?

explanation.summary: This presentation—fever, tonsillar exudates, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and absence of cough—is classic for Group A Streptococcus (GAS) pharyngitis. The absence of viral symptoms (cough, rhinorrhea, conjunctivitis) further supports a bacterial etiology.
explanation.whyCorrect: GAS pharyngitis characteristically presents with fever, sore throat, tonsillar enlargement with purulent exudates, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and notably the absence of cough. These features are part of the Centor criteria used to assess the likelihood of streptococcal pharyngitis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenovirus pharyngitis typically presents with concurrent cough, nasal congestion, and conjunctivitis. The absence of these viral symptoms makes adenovirus less likely.
whyWrongByChoice.B: EBV (infectious mononucleosis) presents with fatigue, malaise, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and sometimes splenomegaly. It is most common in adolescents and young adults, not school-age children with this presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Parainfluenza virus typically causes croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) in children, not isolated pharyngitis with tonsillar exudates. It would present with barking cough and stridor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RSV primarily causes lower respiratory tract infections (bronchiolitis) in infants and young children, not pharyngitis with tonsillar exudates in school-age children.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with sore throat and fever. A rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for Group A Streptococcus is negative. The physician suspects streptococcal pharyngitis despite the negative result. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Obtain a complete blood count with differential

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prescribe ibuprofen and reassure the family

explanation.summary: When the RADT is negative but clinical suspicion for GAS pharyngitis remains high, a throat culture should be obtained. Throat culture has 99% sensitivity for detecting streptococcal infection and is the confirmatory test after a negative RADT.
explanation.whyCorrect: The RADT has approximately 90% sensitivity, meaning up to 10% of true GAS infections may be missed. When clinical suspicion is high despite a negative RADT, a throat culture should be performed because it has 99% sensitivity and serves as the gold standard for GAS detection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Empiric antibiotic treatment without confirmation is not recommended because it exposes the patient to unnecessary drug side effects and contributes to antibiotic resistance if the cause is viral.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A CBC may show leukocytosis in bacterial infection but is nonspecific and does not confirm GAS pharyngitis. Throat culture is the appropriate confirmatory test.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Symptomatic treatment alone is appropriate only if GAS has been ruled out. With high clinical suspicion and a negative RADT, further testing with throat culture is needed before deciding to withhold antibiotics.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titers indicate past streptococcal infection and are used to diagnose post-streptococcal complications (e.g., rheumatic fever), not acute pharyngitis.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents to the emergency department at midnight with a barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarseness. His father reports that the child had a runny nose and low-grade fever for 2 days before the cough started. The child's oxygen saturation is 97%. An anteroposterior neck radiograph is obtained. Which of the following radiographic findings is most likely?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Air-fluid levels in the maxillary sinuses

explanation.summary: This presentation—a toddler with barking cough, stridor, hoarseness, and a preceding URI—is classic for croup (laryngotracheobronchitis). The steeple sign on AP neck radiograph represents subglottic narrowing from mucosal edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: The steeple sign (also called the church spire sign) is the characteristic radiographic finding in croup. It appears on an AP view of the neck as symmetric narrowing of the subglottic airway, creating a pointed appearance like a church steeple, caused by mucosal edema below the vocal cords.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The thumbprint sign is seen on lateral neck radiograph in epiglottitis, where the swollen epiglottis appears thumb-like. Epiglottitis presents with drooling, high fever, and a muffled voice rather than barking cough.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The sail sign refers to an enlarged thymus visible on chest radiograph in young children, which is a normal finding, or can refer to pericardial fat pad sign. It is not associated with croup.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Air-fluid levels in the maxillary sinuses indicate sinusitis, not croup. This child's symptoms are localized to the larynx and trachea.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A widened mediastinum is associated with thoracic aortic pathology, lymphoma, or other mediastinal masses. It is not a finding of laryngotracheobronchitis.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with high fever (40°C / 104°F), drooling, a muffled "hot-potato" voice, and severe respiratory distress. She is sitting in a tripod position and appears toxic. Her parents report that symptoms developed rapidly over several hours. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain a throat culture before any intervention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Secure the airway with endotracheal intubation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform immediate direct laryngoscopy to visualize the epiglottis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administer oral dexamethasone and discharge home

explanation.summary: This presentation—acute high fever, drooling, muffled voice, tripod positioning, and toxic appearance—is classic for epiglottitis, a medical emergency. The immediate priority is securing the airway because the swollen epiglottis can rapidly cause complete airway obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Epiglottitis is a life-threatening emergency where the airway can occlude rapidly. In a child in respiratory distress with signs of epiglottitis, endotracheal intubation should not be delayed. Empiric antibiotics are started after the airway is secured.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Obtaining a throat culture before securing the airway is inappropriate. Oropharyngeal examination can cause agitation and acute airway deterioration in children with epiglottitis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Nebulized racemic epinephrine is used for croup, not epiglottitis. In epiglottitis, the obstruction is supraglottic, and racemic epinephrine targeting subglottic edema would be ineffective.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Direct laryngoscopy in a distressed child with epiglottitis can precipitate complete airway obstruction and cardiorespiratory arrest. Visualization should occur only in a controlled setting with readiness for intubation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oral dexamethasone and discharge is appropriate for mild croup, not epiglottitis. This child has a life-threatening condition requiring immediate airway management and hospital admission.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl is diagnosed with Group A Streptococcus pharyngitis by rapid antigen testing. She has no known drug allergies. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic treatment?

explanation.summary: For GAS pharyngitis in patients without penicillin allergy, a penicillin (usually amoxicillin or penicillin V) is the first-line treatment. These antibiotics are well tolerated, have excellent activity against GAS, and help prevent rheumatic fever.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amoxicillin (or penicillin V) is the first-line antibiotic for GAS pharyngitis because it has excellent activity against Group A Streptococcus, is well tolerated, has a narrow enough spectrum to minimize normal flora disruption, and is effective at preventing the complication of rheumatic fever.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Azithromycin is a second-line option reserved for patients with penicillin allergy. It should not be used first-line because macrolide resistance in GAS exists, and penicillins have superior efficacy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin used when broader coverage is needed (e.g., suspected gonococcal pharyngitis) or when the patient cannot take oral medications. It is unnecessarily broad for uncomplicated GAS pharyngitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Clindamycin is an alternative for penicillin-allergic patients but is not first-line due to its broader spectrum and risk of Clostridioides difficile infection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Doxycycline is not recommended for GAS pharyngitis and is generally avoided in children under 8 years due to risk of tooth discoloration.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 19-year-old college student presents with a 5-day history of fatigue, sore throat, and fever. Physical examination reveals posterior cervical lymphadenopathy and pharyngeal erythema with tonsillar enlargement. A complete blood count shows an absolute lymphocyte count of 6,200/μL with atypical lymphocytes. A heterophile antibody test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infectious mononucleosis due to Epstein-Barr virus

explanation.summary: This young adult presents with the classic triad of infectious mononucleosis: fatigue, pharyngitis, and lymphadenopathy. The increased atypical lymphocytes and positive heterophile test confirm EBV infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Infectious mononucleosis caused by EBV classically presents in adolescents and young adults with fatigue, sore throat, fever, and lymphadenopathy (often posterior cervical). The hallmark laboratory findings are lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes and a positive heterophile antibody test (Monospot).

whyWrongByChoice.A: GAS pharyngitis presents with anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, tonsillar exudates, and absence of cough, but does not cause lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes or a positive heterophile test.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CMV can cause a mononucleosis-like syndrome but typically has a negative heterophile test (heterophile-negative mono). The positive heterophile test points to EBV.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acute HIV infection can present with pharyngitis and lymphadenopathy but would not cause a positive heterophile antibody test. HIV testing (RNA or p24 antigen) would be needed for diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenoviral pharyngitis typically presents with conjunctivitis and upper respiratory symptoms and does not produce a positive heterophile test or the marked atypical lymphocytosis seen here.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy is treated with amoxicillin for Group A Streptococcus pharyngitis. His mother asks which complication antibiotic treatment is most effective at preventing. Which of the following complications is most effectively prevented by antibiotic therapy for GAS pharyngitis?

explanation.summary: Antibiotic treatment of GAS pharyngitis most effectively prevents acute rheumatic fever, an immune-mediated complication that can cause permanent cardiac valve damage. Importantly, antibiotics do not reliably prevent post-infectious glomerulonephritis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The primary reason for treating GAS pharyngitis with antibiotics is to prevent acute rheumatic fever, which involves molecular mimicry between streptococcal M protein and cardiac tissue. Prompt antibiotic therapy within 9 days of symptom onset significantly reduces the risk of this complication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Post-infectious glomerulonephritis is not reliably prevented by antibiotic treatment of GAS pharyngitis. This complication is mediated by immune complex deposition, and antibiotics have not been shown to reduce its incidence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Scarlet fever is caused by streptococcal exotoxins and occurs concurrently with the acute infection. While antibiotics treat the underlying infection, the rash typically appears before treatment is initiated.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reactive arthritis is a post-infectious joint inflammation that occurs after various infections. Prevention of reactive arthritis is not the primary indication for treating GAS pharyngitis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IgA nephropathy is a chronic glomerular disease unrelated to acute streptococcal infection. It is not a recognized complication of GAS pharyngitis.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy with croup has worsening respiratory distress with stridor at rest, subcostal retractions, and tachypnea despite cool mist therapy and oral dexamethasone. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.summary: For croup with worsening respiratory distress despite supportive care and corticosteroids, nebulized racemic epinephrine provides rapid, short-term relief through alpha-1-mediated vasoconstriction that reduces subglottic mucosal edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: Racemic epinephrine acts via alpha-1-receptor-mediated vasoconstriction of the edematous subglottic mucosa, rapidly reducing swelling and improving airflow. Its beta-2 agonist effects also promote bronchodilation. It is the treatment of choice for moderate-to-severe croup not responding to initial therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Albuterol is a beta-2 selective agonist used primarily for bronchospasm (e.g., asthma). It does not have the alpha-1-mediated vasoconstrictive effect needed to reduce the subglottic mucosal edema causing obstruction in croup.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Croup is caused by a virus (most commonly parainfluenza), so antibiotics are not indicated. IV ceftriaxone would be appropriate for bacterial epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis, not viral croup.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intubation is reserved for severe cases with impending respiratory failure that do not respond to racemic epinephrine. It should not be the next step before attempting epinephrine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ipratropium bromide is an anticholinergic bronchodilator used in asthma and COPD. It does not reduce the subglottic mucosal edema that is the primary pathology in croup.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a unilateral sore throat, fever, trismus (difficulty opening the mouth), uvular deviation to the left, and a muffled voice. CT scan of the neck shows a rim-enhancing fluid collection in the right peritonsillar space. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Peritonsillar abscess is a complication of bacterial tonsillitis characterized by unilateral sore throat, fever, trismus, uvular deviation away from the affected side, and a muffled voice. CT imaging confirms a fluid collection in the peritonsillar space.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of unilateral sore throat, trismus, uvular deviation (away from the affected side), muffled voice, and a rim-enhancing peritonsillar fluid collection on CT is classic for peritonsillar abscess. This is the most common complication of bacterial tonsillitis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, stridor, a hot-potato voice, and tripod positioning, but does not cause trismus or uvular deviation. It is a supraglottic process, not a tonsillar one.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Retropharyngeal abscess presents with neck stiffness, dysphagia, and a retropharyngeal fluid collection, typically in younger children. It does not characteristically cause trismus or uvular deviation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ludwig angina is a submandibular space infection typically arising from dental sources, presenting with firm floor-of-mouth swelling and potential airway compromise. It does not cause uvular deviation or peritonsillar fluid collections.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Viral tonsillitis would not produce a rim-enhancing fluid collection on CT, trismus, or uvular deviation. These findings indicate abscess formation, a complication of bacterial infection.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with hoarseness that has persisted for 3 weeks. She reports no sore throat, fever, or recent upper respiratory infection. She has a 20-year history of gastroesophageal reflux disease. Laryngoscopy reveals erythematous, edematous vocal cords. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her laryngitis?

explanation.summary: This patient has chronic hoarseness without infectious symptoms and a long history of GERD. Laryngopharyngeal reflux, in which gastric acid irritates the laryngeal mucosa, is a common noninfectious cause of chronic laryngitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Laryngopharyngeal reflux occurs when gastric contents reach the larynx, causing chronic mucosal irritation, edema, and inflammation of the vocal cords. The absence of infectious symptoms (fever, sore throat, URI) and the history of longstanding GERD make reflux the most likely etiology.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Parainfluenza virus causes acute, self-limited laryngitis that resolves within 1-2 weeks. Three weeks of hoarseness without preceding URI symptoms and no fever makes viral infection unlikely.
whyWrongByChoice.B: GAS pharyngitis presents acutely with fever, sore throat, and tonsillar exudates, not with chronic isolated hoarseness. Bacterial laryngitis from GAS is uncommon.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vocal cord nodules cause hoarseness but appear as bilateral, symmetric nodules on laryngoscopy, not diffuse erythema and edema. They are typically associated with voice overuse in singers or speakers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Candida laryngitis occurs in immunocompromised patients and would present with white patches on the larynx. This immunocompetent patient with GERD more likely has reflux laryngitis.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 5-year-old child is diagnosed with epiglottitis. After the airway is secured with endotracheal intubation, empiric antibiotic therapy is initiated. Which of the following antibiotic regimens provides the most appropriate coverage for the likely causative organisms?

explanation.summary: Epiglottitis requires broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics covering the most common pathogens: H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, GAS, and MRSA. The recommended first-line regimen is IV ceftriaxone (for H. influenzae and other Gram-negatives) plus vancomycin (for MRSA and broad Gram-positive coverage).
explanation.whyCorrect: Given the broad range of causative pathogens in epiglottitis (H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, GAS, and less commonly MRSA), broad-spectrum IV coverage is required. Ceftriaxone provides excellent coverage against H. influenzae and streptococcal species, while vancomycin adds MRSA and additional Gram-positive coverage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oral amoxicillin is appropriate for uncomplicated GAS pharyngitis but is inadequate for epiglottitis, which requires IV antibiotics with broader coverage including H. influenzae and potentially MRSA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oral azithromycin is a second-line option for pharyngitis in penicillin-allergic patients. It does not provide adequate coverage for the range of organisms causing epiglottitis and the IV route is required for this serious infection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Metronidazole provides anaerobic coverage, which is not the primary concern in epiglottitis. The key pathogens (H. influenzae, staphylococci, streptococci) are not covered by metronidazole.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nebulized tobramycin is used for chronic Pseudomonas infections in CF patients. It is not appropriate for acute epiglottitis, which requires systemic IV antibiotics.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl presents with a 3-day history of cough and rhinorrhea followed by the onset of a barking cough and inspiratory stridor tonight. She is well-appearing with oxygen saturation of 96% and only occasional stridor when upset. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?

explanation.summary: This toddler presents with the classic features of croup: a viral prodrome followed by barking cough and inspiratory stridor, peaking at age 3 years during fall/winter months. Parainfluenza virus is the most common cause of croup.
explanation.whyCorrect: Parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3) is responsible for the majority of croup cases. The classic presentation includes a 1-3 day URI prodrome followed by the hallmark barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarseness, typically worse at night.

whyWrongByChoice.A: H. influenzae type b is classically associated with epiglottitis, not croup. Epiglottitis presents with acute high fever, drooling, muffled voice, and toxic appearance without the barking cough of croup.
whyWrongByChoice.B: S. aureus is the most common cause of bacterial tracheitis, which typically follows a viral URI and presents with high fever and purulent secretions. It is not the typical cause of croup.
whyWrongByChoice.D: S. pyogenes (GAS) causes pharyngitis and tonsillitis, not the laryngotracheobronchitis pattern of barking cough and stridor seen in croup.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bordetella pertussis causes whooping cough, characterized by paroxysms of cough followed by an inspiratory whoop, not the barking cough and stridor of croup.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy initially diagnosed with croup develops worsening high fever (40.5°C / 105°F) and thick purulent tracheal secretions after 3 days of illness. He appears toxic and does not improve with racemic epinephrine. Tracheal aspirate culture grows Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Bacterial tracheitis typically occurs as a secondary bacterial infection following a viral URI or croup. It is characterized by high fever, toxic appearance, purulent tracheal secretions, and poor response to croup treatments. S. aureus is the most common causative organism.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bacterial tracheitis classically follows a viral upper respiratory infection and presents with abrupt clinical deterioration, high fever, toxic appearance, and copious purulent secretions. S. aureus is the most commonly implicated pathogen. Unlike croup, it does not respond to corticosteroids or racemic epinephrine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Epiglottitis presents acutely with drooling, muffled voice, and tripod positioning. It does not typically follow a viral prodrome of croup, and the tracheal secretions point to a tracheitis rather than an epiglottic process.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Retropharyngeal abscess presents with neck stiffness, difficulty swallowing, and a retropharyngeal mass, not purulent tracheal secretions growing S. aureus on aspirate culture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspiration pneumonia would present with lower respiratory findings (rales, consolidation on chest X-ray) and typically occurs with a predisposing event such as altered consciousness or dysphagia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Allergic laryngeal edema would present acutely without fever or purulent secretions and would respond to epinephrine. The toxic appearance and positive bacterial culture rule out an allergic etiology.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy develops a diffuse red, papular "sandpaper-like" rash 2 days after the onset of sore throat and fever. On exam, his tongue has a red, bumpy appearance. A rapid strep test is positive. Which of the following best describes the pathogenesis of this rash?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct bacterial invasion of the skin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immune complex deposition in dermal vessels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Drug reaction to the antibiotic prescribed

explanation.summary: This is scarlet fever, which occurs 1-2 days after GAS pharyngitis and is caused by erythrogenic (pyrogenic) exotoxins produced by the streptococcus. The sandpaper-like rash and strawberry tongue are characteristic findings.
explanation.whyCorrect: Scarlet fever is caused by exotoxins (streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxins) produced by GAS. These toxins act as superantigens and cause the characteristic diffuse erythematous, papular, sandpaper-textured rash that can eventually lead to desquamation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: GAS does not directly invade the skin to cause the scarlet fever rash. The rash is toxin-mediated, with the bacteria remaining in the pharynx.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Type IV hypersensitivity involves T-cell-mediated delayed reactions (e.g., contact dermatitis, tuberculin reaction). The scarlet fever rash appears acutely due to circulating exotoxins, not a delayed immune response.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immune complex deposition is the mechanism of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, not scarlet fever. The rash here is a direct effect of streptococcal exotoxins.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The rash appeared 2 days after pharyngitis onset, which is consistent with the natural history of scarlet fever. An antibiotic drug rash would typically occur after starting treatment and would have a different morphology.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of sore throat, nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, cough, and low-grade fever. Physical examination shows pharyngeal erythema with mild tonsillar edema but no exudates. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapid antigen detection test for GAS

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ibuprofen and warm beverages for symptomatic relief

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral dexamethasone to reduce pharyngeal inflammation

explanation.summary: This patient presents with classic viral pharyngitis: sore throat accompanied by nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, and cough, with pharyngeal erythema but no exudates or lymphadenopathy. Viral pharyngitis is treated symptomatically with ibuprofen and warm beverages.
explanation.whyCorrect: The presence of cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea strongly suggests a viral etiology. Viral pharyngitis is diagnosed clinically and managed with supportive care including analgesics (ibuprofen), warm beverages, and throat lozenges. Antibiotics are not indicated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Empiric antibiotics are not appropriate for viral pharyngitis. Antibiotic use without evidence of bacterial infection promotes resistance and exposes the patient to unnecessary side effects.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A rapid strep test is indicated when GAS is suspected (e.g., fever, tonsillar exudates, anterior lymphadenopathy, absence of cough). This patient's symptoms strongly suggest a viral cause, making strep testing unnecessary.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Throat culture is not needed when the clinical picture clearly points to viral pharyngitis. Testing should be reserved for cases where bacterial infection is suspected.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Systemic corticosteroids are not routinely used for uncomplicated viral pharyngitis. Dexamethasone may be used for croup or severe peritonsillar inflammation but not for self-limited viral sore throat.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with poorly controlled HIV (CD4 count 85 cells/μL) presents with painful swallowing and sore throat. Examination of the oropharynx reveals white patches on the pharyngeal mucosa and soft palate. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

explanation.summary: This immunocompromised patient has oropharyngeal candidiasis (thrush), evidenced by white patches in the oropharynx. Localized candidal infections are initially treated with topical oral antifungals such as nystatin or miconazole oral suspension.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fungal pharyngitis from Candida albicans is common in immunocompromised patients, especially those with HIV/AIDS and low CD4 counts. Localized oropharyngeal candidiasis is initially treated with topical antifungal agents such as nystatin oral suspension. Systemic azoles (e.g., fluconazole) are reserved for cases that fail topical therapy or show systemic involvement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acyclovir treats herpes simplex and varicella-zoster viruses, not fungal infections. HSV pharyngitis presents with vesicles and ulcers, not white patches.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IV amphotericin B is reserved for severe, systemic, or refractory fungal infections. It is unnecessarily aggressive for localized oropharyngeal candidiasis and carries significant nephrotoxicity risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amoxicillin treats bacterial infections and would be inappropriate for oropharyngeal candidiasis. In fact, chronic antibiotic use is a risk factor for developing candidal infections.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ceftriaxone is a broad-spectrum antibiotic with no antifungal activity. The white pharyngeal patches in an immunocompromised patient indicate fungal, not bacterial, infection.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: A previously healthy 3-year-old boy presents with a lateral neck radiograph that shows a markedly thickened epiglottis with an enlarged aryepiglottic fold. He has high fever, drooling, and refuses to lie down. The attending physician avoids examining the oropharynx. Which of the following best explains why direct visualization of the epiglottis is avoided in this clinical setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct visualization will cause a vagal response and bradycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Agitation from the examination may precipitate acute airway obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: The oropharyngeal exam is unreliable for diagnosing epiglottitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: The infection may spread to the examiner through aerosolized secretions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tongue depression will push the swollen epiglottis into the trachea

explanation.summary: In children with suspected epiglottitis, direct oropharyngeal examination is avoided because the agitation it causes can worsen respiratory distress and precipitate complete airway obstruction, potentially leading to cardiorespiratory arrest.
explanation.whyCorrect: Children with epiglottitis have critically compromised airways. The agitation and crying triggered by attempting to examine the throat can increase oxygen demand and cause laryngospasm, leading to acute, complete airway obstruction. Cases of cardiorespiratory arrest during attempted visualization have been reported.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While vagal stimulation can occur with airway manipulation, the primary concern is not bradycardia but rather acute airway obstruction from the agitation and distress caused by the examination.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Direct visualization showing a cherry-red, swollen epiglottis is actually a reliable diagnostic finding. The reason it is avoided is patient safety, not diagnostic unreliability.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infection transmission risk is not the primary reason for avoiding the exam. Standard precautions address transmission concerns; the issue is the danger to the child's airway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tongue depression does not mechanically push the epiglottis into the trachea. The concern is that the child's distress response to the examination can cause acute airway compromise.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Pharynx, Larynx, and Trachea
questions.stem: An 8-year-old girl is diagnosed with GAS pharyngitis. She has a documented anaphylactic reaction to penicillin. Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic for this patient?
