whyWrongByChoice.D: Posaconazole has activity against Aspergillus and is used for prophylaxis and salvage therapy but is not the first-line treatment for invasive aspergillosis.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Nasal Cavities and Sinuses
questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl presents with unilateral foul-smelling purulent nasal discharge for 5 days. She has no fever or systemic symptoms. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals erythematous nasal mucosa with purulent discharge on the right side. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

explanation.summary: Unilateral foul-smelling purulent nasal discharge in a young child is the classic presentation of a nasal foreign body. Children frequently insert small objects into their noses, and the resulting obstruction and secondary infection produce the characteristic malodorous unilateral discharge.
explanation.whyCorrect: A nasal foreign body is the most common cause of unilateral, foul-smelling purulent nasal discharge in a young child without systemic symptoms. The foreign body causes local inflammation, obstruction, and secondary bacterial infection producing the malodorous discharge. Anterior rhinoscopy or nasal endoscopy with foreign body removal is the treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute bacterial sinusitis typically presents with bilateral symptoms, facial pain or pressure, and often follows a viral URI. Unilateral foul-smelling discharge without systemic symptoms in a child suggests a foreign body.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Allergic rhinitis presents with bilateral clear, watery discharge, sneezing, and nasal itching. It does not cause unilateral purulent, foul-smelling discharge.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Choanal atresia is a congenital condition typically diagnosed in the neonatal period with bilateral nasal obstruction and feeding difficulties. It does not present with unilateral purulent discharge at age 6.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nasal polyps are uncommon in children this age unless associated with cystic fibrosis. They present with bilateral nasal obstruction and hyposmia, not unilateral foul-smelling discharge.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Nasal Cavities and Sinuses
questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with chronic rhinosinusitis undergoes CT scanning that reveals complete opacification of the right maxillary sinus with an air-fluid level in the left maxillary sinus. Which of the following findings on CT is most indicative of severe chronic sinusitis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Air-fluid level in the left maxillary sinus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deviated nasal septum to the right

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mucosal thickening of the ethmoid sinuses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normal aeration of the frontal sinuses

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sinus opacification of the right maxillary sinus

explanation.summary: Sinus opacification — complete filling of a sinus cavity with fluid or inflammatory tissue — is a hallmark finding of severe chronic sinusitis on CT scanning. Other typical CT findings include mucosal thickening and air-fluid levels, but complete opacification indicates more severe disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sinus opacification occurs when the sinus is completely filled with fluid, polyps, or thickened mucosa, leaving no visible air space. This represents the most severe CT finding in chronic sinusitis, indicating complete obstruction of sinus drainage and advanced disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An air-fluid level indicates fluid partially filling a sinus and can be seen in both acute and chronic sinusitis. It is less indicative of severe disease than complete opacification.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A deviated nasal septum is an anatomic variant that may predispose to sinusitis by obstructing drainage but is not itself a sign of sinusitis severity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mucosal thickening is a common finding in chronic sinusitis but represents less severe disease than complete sinus opacification.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Normal aeration of the frontal sinuses indicates that those sinuses are not affected and is a normal finding.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Nasal Cavities and Sinuses
questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with nasal congestion that worsens when she enters cold environments or is exposed to strong perfumes. She denies sneezing, nasal itching, or watery eyes. Skin prick testing is negative for common allergens. Nasal mucosa appears erythematous but not pale. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Nonallergic rhinitis is triggered by environmental irritants such as cold air, perfumes, odors, chemicals, and air pollutants. Unlike allergic rhinitis, it does not involve IgE-mediated hypersensitivity, and allergy testing is negative.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has rhinitis triggered by cold temperatures and perfumes with negative allergy testing, consistent with nonallergic (vasomotor) rhinitis. Common triggers include cold temperature, low humidity, perfumes, chemicals, and air pollutants. The erythematous (rather than pale) nasal mucosa and absence of allergic symptoms (itching, sneezing, watery eyes) further support a nonallergic etiology.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated type I hypersensitivity reaction with positive allergy testing. This patient's negative skin prick testing and lack of classic allergic symptoms (sneezing, itching, watery eyes) argue against this diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Chronic sinusitis requires symptoms lasting more than 12 weeks, typically with facial pressure, purulent discharge, and CT findings. This patient's symptoms are triggered by specific environmental exposures.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nasal polyposis presents with progressive bilateral nasal obstruction and hyposmia. It is not specifically triggered by cold air or perfumes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rhinitis medicamentosa is caused by overuse of topical nasal decongestants. There is no history of decongestant use in this patient.

questions.topicName: Inflammatory Disorders of the Nasal Cavities and Sinuses
questions.stem: A physician is counseling a patient with allergic rhinitis about available treatments. The patient asks about a medication that prevents mast cell degranulation. However, the physician explains that this agent is rarely used because of its disadvantages compared to intranasal corticosteroids. Which of the following medications is the physician describing?

explanation.summary: Cromolyn sodium is a mast cell stabilizer that prevents histamine release by inhibiting mast cell degranulation. It is rarely used for allergic rhinitis due to inferior efficacy, slow onset of action (4-7 days), and the need for frequent dosing (3-4 times daily) compared to intranasal corticosteroids.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cromolyn sodium stabilizes mast cell membranes and prevents the release of histamine and other mediators when patients are exposed to allergens. Despite this mechanism, it is rarely prescribed because intranasal corticosteroids have better efficacy, faster onset (hours to days vs 4-7 days), and more convenient dosing (1-2 times daily vs 3-4 times daily).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cetirizine is a second-generation antihistamine that blocks H1 receptors on target cells. It does not prevent mast cell degranulation; rather, it blocks the effects of already-released histamine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fluticasone is an intranasal corticosteroid that reduces inflammation broadly by downregulating inflammatory cytokines. It is the first-line therapy being compared to, not the agent described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Montelukast is a leukotriene receptor antagonist that blocks the effects of leukotrienes. It does not prevent mast cell degranulation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oxymetazoline is a topical alpha-adrenergic agonist nasal decongestant that causes vasoconstriction. It has no effect on mast cell degranulation.
